tool_use_result.stdout:
59:   - **Per-run working-tree isolation (E22.X / #1137, local loop only).** Immediately after the prompt fetch resolves `decomposed_from_run_id` — the first point the runner learns its lineage — `runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/worktree.go` provisions a **lineage-keyed git worktree** and relocates `cfg.workingDir` into it, so concurrent runs driven on one local host no longer share a single working tree (the hosted GitHub Actions path is already per-job-isolated by `actions/checkout`). The worktree is a detached checkout of HEAD at `<git-common-dir>/fishhawk-worktrees/run-<root>`, where `root` is `lineageRoot(runID, decomposedFromRunID)` = the **parent** run id for a decomposed child (so all siblings share one lineage worktree) or the run's **own** id for a solo run (its own worktree). Under ADR-041 (E24.1 / #1141) each child no longer layers its commit onto a single shared `fishhawk/run-<parent>` branch; instead each cuts and pushes its **own sole-writer slice branch** `fishhawk/run-<parent>/slice-<n>` (n = the child's `slice_index`, the sub_plan position). The shared lineage worktree sequentially checks out each distinct slice branch in turn — E24.1 retains **serial** child dispatch (the lineage lockfile serializes same-lineage stages, so git never sees one branch checked out in two worktrees at once; parallel dispatch is E24.3+). Fan-in of the slice branches onto the consolidated branch is ADR-041 / E24.2, out of scope here. The common gitdir is resolved via `git rev-parse --git-common-dir` (not `--git-dir`) so an operator checkout that is itself a linked worktree still lands under the one shared root. Because every downstream git op re-derives `repoDir := cfg.workingDir` (the FreshFetchBase / checkoutChildBase / commit / verify-gate sequences), relocating that single field isolates the whole stage with no per-block change. An O_EXCL lockfile beside the worktree (`run-<root>.lock`) serializes same-lineage stages and **fails loud** (category-A) if a live process already holds it — decomposition drives a lineage's stages sequentially, so a concurrent same-lineage stage is a corruption hazard, not a legitimate state; a lock left by a crashed prior stage (pid no longer alive) is reclaimed. The worktree lives under `.git`, invisible to the operator's `git status`. This is the **`working_dir`-means-repo-root-to-derive-from** semantic shift (previously `working_dir` *was* the run's tree) and the realization of **ADR-035's branch-ownership invariant extended to tree-ownership**. Teardown is reclamation-by-lazy-sweep at the next provision: `sweepTerminalWorktrees` (run at provision start, before the new worktree is added) enumerates `git worktree list`, and for each `run-<root>` under `fishhawk-worktrees/` reads the full lineage-root run id from a `run-<root>.runid` sidecar (written at provision beside the worktree, since the short dir name can't be resolved back to a run id) and asks the backend whether that lineage is terminal via the additive `lineage_complete` field on `GET /v0/runs/{id}` (`upload.Client.RunLineageComplete`). `lineage_complete` is true when the lineage-root run AND every decomposed child are terminal (computed in `backend/internal/server/reads.go` from the `decomposed_from` child graph). **fishhawkd is the authority on lineage terminality only** (it can't reach the operator's filesystem); the runner performs the physical `git worktree remove --force` host-side. So a worktree for a terminal lineage lingers until the next provision on that host — acceptable: it lives under `.git`, is invisible to `git status`, and is cheap. The sweep is entirely best-effort (a missing sidecar, backend error, or git error logs a degradation event and is skipped; it never fails the stage and never removes a worktree it can't prove reclaimable). Relocating the run's commit off the operator checkout's HEAD broke the MCP server's `diff_summary` (which shelled `git show --numstat HEAD` in `working_dir`); the numstat totals now ride on the `git_diff` wire event (`insertions`/`deletions`) and `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_stage.go` reads them from there, keeping the MCP server worktree- and decomposition-unaware. **Parallel children (E24.4 / #1144).** A default-off runner flag `--parallel-isolate` (`runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/flags.go`) inverts the child keying: when set, `lineageRoot(runID, decomposedFromRunID, parallelIsolate)` returns the **child's own** id, so each concurrent sibling provisions an **isolated** `run-<child>` worktree (its own lock + `.runid` sidecar) instead of racing the one shared `run-<parent>` tree — the correct shape now that each child owns a distinct sole-writer slice branch (E24.1). The serial/solo paths are byte-for-byte unchanged when the flag is off. The MCP tool `fishhawk_run_children` (`backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_children.go`) is the driver that sets it: it reads the parent's `plan_decomposed` audit entry (`child_run_ids` + `effective_max_parallel`, 0 = unlimited), spawns each **pending** child's implement stage concurrently — `--parallel-isolate` appended — under an `errgroup` bounded by the orchestrator-resolved effective cap (clamp-DOWN-only against an optional `max_parallel` override), and **awaits ALL with no sibling-cancel** (a child failure is data in the consolidated per-child result, never a tool error; re-invocation is idempotent — only `pending` children are dispatched). The spawn/parse/SIGKILL-group core is the single `spawnRunnerStage` helper shared with `fishhawk_run_stage`. Per-child SIGKILL crash-survival is inherited from the same stale-lock-reclaim + lazy-sweep path (a child has no children, so its own terminality == `lineage_complete`).
60:   - **Pre-push compile gate (#728).** On the standalone (non-decomposed) PR-opening path, after the scope-only commit is created and **before** the push, the runner (`runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/main.go::verifyCommittedTreeCompiles`, wired via `gitops.CommitAndPushArgs.VerifyCommit`) compiles the *committed* tree in an isolated `git worktree --detach` at the new HEAD SHA — not the working tree, which still carries the excluded scope-drift on disk — and runs `go vet ./...` per `go.work` module. `go vet` typechecks test files (which `go build` skips), so it catches the interface-ripple class where a scope-bounded commit drops a build-required conformance stub left in a test file (`does not implement <iface> (missing method ...)`). The block condition (#959) is **any clean compiler/typecheck diagnostic line** — `(vet: )?file.go:LINE:COL: message` (`goDiagnosticBlocks`), with the `compileDiagnosticMarkers` substring allowlist kept only as a backstop — unless every such line is a recognized dependency-resolution form (`no required module provides`, `missing go.sum entry`, `cannot find module/package`, `cannot query module`; dep failures print in the same `file.go:LINE:COL:` shape under a cold cache/offline, so the exclusion is load-bearing). The marker-allowlist-only era chased the open-ended typecheck-message tail one production bug at a time (#774, then #959's `unknown field ... in struct literal` skipping as `vet_nonzero_non_compile` and pushing an uncompilable head on run 07bce059). A real compile failure fails the stage **category B** (`gitops.ErrCommitWouldNotCompile`, re-scope/re-plan) with the drift file list, and because it runs pre-push, origin is untouched — no non-compiling PR is opened. Every infrastructure case (no drift, non-Go repo, no Go toolchain, worktree/`go work edit` failure, a nonzero exit with no parseable diagnostic, or dep-resolution-only diagnostics) is a non-blocking skip (`compile_gate_skipped`), degrading to the prior "open the PR" behavior — but a skip now requires evidence the toolchain/dependency infrastructure failed, not merely an unrecognized diagnostic message; a pure vet analyzer finding consequently blocks too (CI's golangci-lint bundles govet, and a block routes to `verify_fix_reinvoke` rather than a dead stage). Decomposed children are intentionally ungated (a child may legitimately not compile before later children land); gating the consolidated decomposition tip is a follow-up.
103:**Push-and-open-pr implement advancement invariant** (#742): mirroring the plan-stage gate, an implement stage that will open a PR has its terminal transition driven by the **`/pull-request` upload** (`handleShipPullRequest`), **not** by trace upload alone. The runner ships the trace *before* it commits/pushes/opens the PR, so advancing to `awaiting_approval` on trace upload would strand the run at the review gate with a null `pull_request_url` if the PR-open step then fails (the b6811dc9 zombie). The runner stamps `push_and_open_pr` in the bundle manifest (`bundle.ManifestData` / `bundle.Manifest`, both `omitempty`, lockstep across the two modules — see the trace-bundle manifest row in §10) for every standalone implement stage (not `--no-pr`, not a decomposed child). `advanceStageAfterTrace` (`trace.go::pushAndOpenPRGated`) then leaves the stage in `running` when the flag is set **and** the bundle carries a non-empty `git_diff` (a commit + PR-open will follow) — emitting the `runtime_observed` calibration row before the early return, since the bundle timing is only available at trace time. The `/pull-request` upload becomes the authoritative terminal driver: the **success** body advances `running → awaiting_approval`/`succeeded` (+ orchestrator `Advance` for a gateless stage) after stamping `run.pull_request_url` (`advanceImplementStageAfterPR`); the **failure** body (`{outcome:"failed", category, reason}`, `failPullRequestStage`) fails the stage (category `C` retryable / `B` re-scope) + advances + appends a `pull_request_failed` audit entry, so the run never reaches review with a null PR. The runner reports that failure from the implement post-processing block (`main.go::reportPullRequestFailure`, gated on the same standalone-implement condition) when `openPRAndShipArtifact` errors — unifying #728's `ErrCommitWouldNotCompile` (category B) into this report path. An **empty** diff is the no-changes carve-out: the agent made no edits, opens no PR, and never POSTs `/pull-request`, so `pushAndOpenPRGated` returns false and the stage advances via the trace handler as before (gating it would hang the stage in `running`). Non-implement stages and a false flag (older bundles, `--no-pr`) are byte-identical to the prior trace-driven advance. **Decomposed-child push gate (#771)**: a decomposed child opens no PR (the parent run opens one consolidated PR, ADR-032) but still commits + pushes onto the shared branch *after* the trace ships, so the same zombie hole applied — a child push failure left the child stage at terminal `succeeded` with no code on the shared branch, which the childcompletion sweeper would then consolidate into a PR silently missing that child's work. The runner stamps a separate `push_to_shared_branch` manifest flag (mutually exclusive with `push_and_open_pr`) for decomposed children; `trace.go::childPushGated` mirrors `pushAndOpenPRGated` to leave the child stage in `running`, and the `/pull-request` upload drives the terminal transition via a new `{outcome:"pushed", branch, head_sha, base_sha}` success variant (`succeedChildPushStage`: running → terminal + `child_pushed` audit entry, no PR artifact) or the existing `{outcome:"failed"}` variant on a push failure (category `C` retryable via the #698 parent-park). **Fix-up push gate (#794)**: a fix-up re-dispatch (#762) opens no new PR (it commits onto the EXISTING PR branch *after* the trace ships) but the same zombie hole applied in its worst form — a fix-up stamped *neither* `push_and_open_pr` *nor* `push_to_shared_branch`, so it terminalized at trace upload *before* its commit/push, and a push or `#728` compile-gate failure was silently swallowed: the stage stayed `succeeded`, the implement re-review approved an *unlanded* diff, and the #788 fix-up recovery never fired (the stage never went terminal-failed). The runner now stamps a third mutually-exclusive `push_fixup` manifest flag for fix-up passes; `trace.go::fixupPushGated` mirrors `childPushGated` to leave the fix-up stage in `running` (the advisory implement re-review still fires at trace time on the bundle diff — only the TERMINAL transition is deferred), and the `/pull-request` upload drives the terminal transition via a new `{outcome:"fixup_pushed", branch, head_sha, base_sha}` success variant (`succeedFixupPushStage`: running → terminal + `fixup_pushed` audit entry, no PR artifact, idempotency-guarded on `(stage_id, head_sha)`) or the existing `{outcome:"failed"}` variant on a push/compile-gate failure — which now genuinely reaches `failPullRequestStage → maybeRecoverFixupFailure`, restoring the run to its pre-fix-up review gate with the original mergeable PR intact. **Dangling-PR close on a gating implement-review reject (#877)**: a gating agent implement-review (`reviewers.agent>0`, `human==0`) runs SYNCHRONOUSLY during the raw-trace upload (`advanceStageAfterTrace::runImplementReviews`) and fails the implement stage category-B with reason `implement_review_rejected: …` (hoisted to the const `implementReviewGatingRejectReason`) BEFORE the runner — which has no view of that verdict — opens its PR and POSTs `/pull-request`. By then the stage is terminally failed, so `handleShipPullRequest` records the PR artifact + `pull_request_opened` audit + URL backfill (the honest open) but `advanceImplementStageAfterPR` no-ops (stage not running), leaving the rejected change with an open PR that will never merge. After the backfill and BEFORE the lineage guard, the handler detects that exact state — implement + `failed` + category-B + the `implementReviewGatingRejectPrefix` reason prefix (same source of truth as the trace failure site; other category-B sources are excluded) — and calls `closePRAfterGatingReject`: it posts a short explanatory PR comment, closes the PR via the new `githubclient.ClosePullRequest` (`PATCH .../pulls/{number}` body `{"state":"closed"}`, mirroring `ForceUpdateRef`), and writes a `pull_request_closed_after_review_reject` audit entry (system actor, payload `{run_id, stage_id, artifact_id, pr_number, pr_url, failure_reason}`). Fail-open throughout: GitHub unconfigured, a nil `installation_id`, an unparseable repo, or a close error WARNs and skips — the stage is already failed, so a failed close must never 500 the handler. Advisory reviews dispatch detached and never reject-fail the stage, so they never reach this state and are byte-identical (PR-open timing for the advisory flow is unchanged; ADR-036/#876 enforces at the landing gate, not at PR-open).
117:**Audit category `plan_review_started`** (#600): a single best-effort entry emitted by `runPlanReviews` at dispatch — **only** on the branch where a reviewer will actually run (`reviewers.agent > 0` AND a `PlanReviewer` is wired), never for the gateless (`agent == 0`) or nil-reviewer (`plan_review_skipped`) branches. Payload shape `ReviewStartedPayload{ConfiguredAgents, Authority}` (`backend/internal/planreview/review.go`). It is emitted synchronously before the per-reviewer loop, so its audit sequence always precedes every `plan_reviewed` entry under both gating (synchronous) and advisory (detached) authority. This is the MCP-readable proxy that lets a consumer distinguish *configured + running* (a `started` entry with no terminal entry) from *none configured* (no entry at all) without re-fetching the workflow spec — the basis for the `review_status` = `pending` state surfaced by `fishhawk_get_run_status` / `fishhawk_get_plan` and waited on by `fishhawk_await_review` (see the MCP row in §10 "Where to look"). **Completeness is count-gated (#1127)**: the payload's `ConfiguredAgents` is the threshold the MCP `review_status` resolves `complete` against — `review_status` stays `pending` until `landed_terminal >= configured_agents` (ANY terminal kind — `plan_reviewed` / `plan_review_failed` / `plan_review_skipped` — counts toward the round), the SAME completeness rule `checkPlanReviewSettled` uses for the approval gate, so `await_review` / `get_run_status` no longer report a partial heterogeneous result (a poll that catches the window after the first of N reviewers landed but before the rest finish now reports `pending`, not `complete` with one verdict). An absent or non-positive `ConfiguredAgents` (old/malformed payload) degrades to the prior complete-on-first-verdict predicate.
211:**Gating consolidated review on the parent (#1060)**: per-child reviews are advisory-to-the-slice early signal — none of them sees the *consolidated* diff that actually merges, so a child-raised high never gated the parent merge and drive advanced `review:awaiting_approval → awaiting_merge` on vacuously-terminal review evidence (zero reviewers ever ran against the parent). Two coupled pieces close this. (1) **Drive safety** (slice 1): `implementReviewRound` now resolves the configured agent count from the spec even when no `implement_review_started` round was dispatched, so `ObserveParkedReviewForDrive` can distinguish a genuinely reviewer-less run (`configured==0`, vacuously terminal — still advances, never wedges) from a parent with reviewers configured but no round yet dispatched (`configured>0 && !started`, non-terminal — parks instead of stamping `checks_green_awaiting_merge`). (2) **Consolidated dispatch** (slice 2): when `orchestrator.Advance` dispatches a decomposed parent's review stage WITH the consolidated PR present, it fires `ConsolidatedReviewDispatcher` (interface field on the orchestrator, satisfied server-side by `*server.Server` to keep the dependency direction `server→orchestrator` and avoid an import cycle). `Server.DispatchConsolidatedReview` sources the consolidated diff via `githubclient.ComparePatch(base=DefaultRef, head=consolidatedBranch(parentID))` and runs `runImplementReviews` against it with the stageID of the **parent's implement stage** (the `awaiting_children→succeeded` stage). That stage id is the load-bearing seam: the `implement_review_started`/`implement_reviewed` round and any concerns attach there, so the round is non-vacuous for the drive gate AND `fishhawk_fixup_stage` on that stage resolves the consolidated concern (same `(run, stage)` concern store, no new fix-up code path). The dispatch is best-effort + idempotent (runs detached on `bgReviews`; `runImplementReviews`' started-key guard dedups the sweeper/event-driven double-advance) and no-ops for ordinary runs (a `decomposed_from==nil` run with a PR but no children). A GitHub-truncated consolidated diff (the large-diff fan-out case — the 300-file cap or an omitted oversized patch) is surfaced loudly: a WARN log plus a durable `consolidated_review_diff_truncated` audit entry (system actor, payload `{reason, changed_files}`, attached to the parent implement stage), and the review still runs on the partial diff rather than aborting. **Parent fix-up re-invokes the shared branch (#1063)**: a fix-up on a decomposed parent's implement stage re-opens it to `pending`, where `Advance` re-enters `fanoutIfDecomposed`. That helper is now guarded on existing children — when the parent already has minted children (a `ListRuns(DecomposedFrom==parent, Limit:1)` probe returns ≥1), it skips the fan-out and returns `(false, nil)` so `Advance` falls through to `dispatchStage` and re-invokes the parent's implement stage against the existing shared branch rather than re-minting a fresh fan-out. The fix-up branch derivation matches: `server.fixupBranchForRun` resolves a decomposed PARENT (`DecomposedFrom==nil` WITH minted children) to the consolidated/shared branch `fishhawk/run-<shortID(runID)>` (byte-matching `orchestrator.consolidatedBranch`) instead of the per-stage branch, so the fix-up commit lands on the consolidated PR head. Only the first fan-out (zero children) mints; the concern-attach + resolve seam is unchanged.
227:**Audit category `implement_reviewed`**: one entry per invocation, payload shape `ImplementReviewedPayload{ReviewerKind, ReviewerModel, Authority, Verdict, Concerns[], FreeForm}` (identical to `PlanReviewedPayload`). The base-rebase re-invoke supplemental pass (ADR-042 / #1250) adds two `omitempty` provenance fields `Origin` (`base_rebase_reinvoke`) + `HeadSHA` (the re-landed head) — absent and byte-identical on the first review and the parent-decomposition consolidated review; they label the additive supplemental verdict and form its `(stage_id, Origin, HeadSHA)` idempotency key. They are retained for dedup (and future labeling); #1250 adds NO `get_run_status`/`await_review` rendering surface for them. This is an internal audit category, not an issue-comment surface (no `docs/issue-comment-surfaces.md` entry). Surfaced by `fishhawk_get_run_status` (MCP) in `GetRunStatusOutput.ImplementReviews[]`.
340:## 10. Where to look
360:| Claude Code MCP server | `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/` (separate binary built from the backend module per ADR-021 / #322). v0 tool set: four read-only — `fishhawk_get_active_run` (E19.3 / #343), `fishhawk_get_plan` (E19.4 / #344; D2 / #476: also surfaces predicted_runtime_minutes, predicted_runtime_confidence, and decomposition.sub_plans when present), `fishhawk_get_run_status` (E19.5 / #345), `fishhawk_list_audit` (E19.6 / #346) — plus the lifecycle-write surface (E22 / #389): `fishhawk_start_run` (E22.1 / #390), `fishhawk_resume_run` (E22.X / #978: category-B recovery — pointed at a top-level run it mints a plan-stage-less child re-executing the parent's approved plan; pointed at a failed decomposition CHILD it re-drives that child IN PLACE on the shared parent branch (#1081), not a new run — both fold operator-named `add_scope_files` as a pre-approved #961 amendment; wraps `POST /v0/runs/{run_id}/recover`), `fishhawk_cancel_run` (E22.2 / #391), `fishhawk_retry_stage` (E22.3 / #392), `fishhawk_approve_plan` + `fishhawk_reject_plan` (E22.4 / #393), `fishhawk_list_runs` (E22.5 / #394), `fishhawk_fixup_stage` (E22.X / #762: routes advisory implement-review concerns back to the agent for a bounded, operator-gated fix-up pass; wraps `POST /v0/stages/{id}/fixup`), `fishhawk_revise_plan` (E22.X / #1099: the plan-gate revise verdict — re-plans IN PLACE against a binding operator design constraint, the plan-stage analogue of fix-up; wraps `POST /v0/stages/{id}/revise`) — plus the calibration read tool `fishhawk_runtime_calibration` (#470: agents call before writing a plan; wraps `GET /v0/calibration`) — plus the review-lifecycle read tool `fishhawk_await_review` (#600: blocks until a stage's agent review reaches a terminal state) — plus the sequence-anchored audit await primitive `fishhawk_await_audit` (#962: blocks until the next audit entry with a given category and sequence > `since_sequence` lands; see below). Phase A closes with E22.6 (docs sweep + E2E test extension). **Review lifecycle on the MCP surface (#600)**: `fishhawk_get_run_status` adds `plan_review_status` + `implement_review_status` and `fishhawk_get_plan` adds `plan_review_status` — each a `ReviewStatus{Stage, Status, Reviews[], PollIntervalSeconds}` where `Status` is `none` \| `pending` \| `complete` \| `skipped` \| `failed`, derived entirely from the audit trail. **Completeness is count-gated (#1127)**: the round is terminal only once `landed_terminal >= configured_agents` (the latest `*_review_started` entry's `ConfiguredAgents`; ANY terminal kind — `reviewed`/`review_failed`/`review_skipped` — counts), the SAME rule `checkPlanReviewSettled`/`checkImplementReviewSettled` use for the approval/merge gates, so a poll catching the heterogeneous partial-landing window (reviewers run sequentially, each minutes long) reports `pending` rather than `complete` with only the first reviewer's verdict. Once the count threshold is met, precedence resolves the status (`reviewed → complete`, else `review_skipped → skipped`, else `review_failed → failed`) and `Reviews[]` is the UNION of every decoded terminal row — one per configured reviewer. Below the threshold (or with no terminal entry) the status is `review_started → pending`, else `none`. An absent/non-positive `ConfiguredAgents` (old/malformed started payload) degrades to the prior complete-on-first-verdict predicate so the surface never strands on `pending`. The `pending` state (a `*_review_started` entry with fewer than `configured_agents` terminal entries) is the gap the existing `Reviews[]`/`ImplementReviews[]` slices couldn't express — it subsumes a still-running review, a silently-failed/timed-out one, and the partial-landing window. The existing `Reviews[]`/`ImplementReviews[]` fields stay populated unchanged (additive, no driver regression). **Poll-authoritative contract (#879)**: re-polling `fishhawk_get_run_status` is the AUTHORITATIVE way to reach a terminal review status; on the `pending` status the `ReviewStatus` carries a server-suggested `poll_interval_seconds` (15s) — a scalar int, #371-safe — so a polling agent stops guessing sleeps. The hint is populated ONLY on `pending` (the one state worth re-polling) and rides in via the shared `ReviewStatus` to every poll surface (`fishhawk_get_run_status`, `fishhawk_get_plan`) from one edit. `fishhawk_await_review` (input `{run_id, stage, timeout_seconds}`, default **360s** — recalibrated from 120s to exceed the measured 3.5–4.5min review latency and the 300s reviewer budget (#878), cap 600s) is demoted to an OPTIONAL convenience block over that poll: it returns immediately on a terminal/none status and otherwise polls the existing `GET /v0/runs/{id}/audit` endpoint server-side on an injectable interval (no new backend long-poll, no wall-clock sleeps in tests) until a terminal entry lands, the run itself reaches a terminal state (the **ADR-036 #874 non-stranding backstop** — an inline `succeeded`/`failed`/`cancelled` comparison against the fishhawk-mcp-local `Run.State`, so a verdict that will never land never holds the session open), or the timeout fires. It is idempotent/resumable: a `pending`-on-timeout result carries the `poll_interval_seconds` hint plus an actionable message framing the re-call (or a switch to `get_run_status` polling) as the documented next step and naming `FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_TIMEOUT`. A 360s synchronous call may still hit a client/transport per-call timeout — acceptable precisely because poll-the-handle is the blessed primary path and a cut-short await is a no-op the caller can re-issue. **Native MCP Tasks (`invocationMode:async`) is a deferred follow-up** — the pinned go-sdk has no Tasks symbols; this ships the sync/poll fallback only. Implementation: `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/review.go` (`reviewStatusFor`, shared `decodeReviewVerdicts`/`decodeSkippedReviews`, `awaitReview` handler). **Sequence-anchored audit await (#962)**: `fishhawk_await_audit` (input `{run_id, category, since_sequence, timeout_seconds}`, same 360s-default/600s-cap clamp as await_review) is the await PRIMITIVE beneath the category-specific waits — it blocks until the next audit entry with the given category and sequence STRICTLY GREATER than `since_sequence` lands and returns that entry (status `found`); a timeout returns a gapless re-arm anchor (`latest_sequence` == `since_sequence` when nothing landed, so re-calling from it cannot skip an entry), and the same ADR-036 run-terminal backstop resolves the wait after ONE final anchored read (status `run_terminal`, distinct from `timeout` because some categories land at/after the terminal transition and the final read must win). Anchoring at the `fixup_pushed` entry's sequence makes the #894 stale-pre-fix-up-verdict read impossible by construction. Beneath it, `GET /v0/runs/{id}/audit` gains an additive `since_sequence` query param (strictly-greater filter applied before pagination, in-memory like the #215 `stage_id` filter — `handleListRunAudit` in `backend/internal/server/reads.go`). `fishhawk_await_review` is unchanged: its #894 fix-up-boundary floor lives in `reviewStatusFor`. Implementation: `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/await_audit.go` (`awaitAudit`, `nextAuditEntry`, `awaitAuditRunTerminalBackstop`), reusing `clampAwaitTimeout` / `reviewPollInterval` / `runStateIsTerminal`; cross-boundary seam test at `backend/internal/integration/mcp/await_audit_test.go`. **Stage-execution wait contract ([ADR-037](https://github.com/kuhlman-labs/fishhawk/issues/879) / #880)**: the execution-lifecycle twin of the review-status contract — `fishhawk_get_run_status` adds `plan_stage_wait_status` + `implement_stage_wait_status`, each a `StageWaitStatus{Stage, Status, PollIntervalSeconds}` where `Status` is `pending` \| `running` \| `succeeded` \| `failed` \| `cancelled` derived from the durable `(run_id, stage_id)` handle's stage row, and `fishhawk_run_stage` adds `stage_wait_status` on its post-run output. Re-polling `get_run_status` is the AUTHORITATIVE wait; while non-terminal the status carries a server-suggested `poll_interval_seconds` of **30s** (coarser than reviews' 15s — stages run minutes), dropped once the run itself is terminal (the ADR-036 #874 non-stranding backstop, reusing `runStateIsTerminal`). The synchronous-with-progress `fishhawk_run_stage` call is the negotiated fallback. The SDK-independent non-blocking dispatch (#1232, `fishhawk_dispatch_stage` / `dispatch_stage.go`) ships the immediate-`(run_id, stage_id)`-handle + poll-to-terminal UX TODAY — it spawns the runner detached (own process group, output → a per-run log FILE not a pipe, a reaper goroutine collects the exit) and returns the handle plus a non-terminal `stage_wait_status` at once, so a SINGLE MCP session can poll `fishhawk_get_run_status` to terminal AND decide a mid-stage scope amendment in-band via `fishhawk_decide_scope_amendment` between polls (the #1189 fix). This **supersedes the interim `fishhawk run auto-decide` second chann

content.content:
59:   - **Per-run working-tree isolation (E22.X / #1137, local loop only).** Immediately after the prompt fetch resolves `decomposed_from_run_id` — the first point the runner learns its lineage — `runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/worktree.go` provisions a **lineage-keyed git worktree** and relocates `cfg.workingDir` into it, so concurrent runs driven on one local host no longer share a single working tree (the hosted GitHub Actions path is already per-job-isolated by `actions/checkout`). The worktree is a detached checkout of HEAD at `<git-common-dir>/fishhawk-worktrees/run-<root>`, where `root` is `lineageRoot(runID, decomposedFromRunID)` = the **parent** run id for a decomposed child (so all siblings share one lineage worktree) or the run's **own** id for a solo run (its own worktree). Under ADR-041 (E24.1 / #1141) each child no longer layers its commit onto a single shared `fishhawk/run-<parent>` branch; instead each cuts and pushes its **own sole-writer slice branch** `fishhawk/run-<parent>/slice-<n>` (n = the child's `slice_index`, the sub_plan position). The shared lineage worktree sequentially checks out each distinct slice branch in turn — E24.1 retains **serial** child dispatch (the lineage lockfile serializes same-lineage stages, so git never sees one branch checked out in two worktrees at once; parallel dispatch is E24.3+). Fan-in of the slice branches onto the consolidated branch is ADR-041 / E24.2, out of scope here. The common gitdir is resolved via `git rev-parse --git-common-dir` (not `--git-dir`) so an operator checkout that is itself a linked worktree still lands under the one shared root. Because every downstream git op re-derives `repoDir := cfg.workingDir` (the FreshFetchBase / checkoutChildBase / commit / verify-gate sequences), relocating that single field isolates the whole stage with no per-block change. An O_EXCL lockfile beside the worktree (`run-<root>.lock`) serializes same-lineage stages and **fails loud** (category-A) if a live process already holds it — decomposition drives a lineage's stages sequentially, so a concurrent same-lineage stage is a corruption hazard, not a legitimate state; a lock left by a crashed prior stage (pid no longer alive) is reclaimed. The worktree lives under `.git`, invisible to the operator's `git status`. This is the **`working_dir`-means-repo-root-to-derive-from** semantic shift (previously `working_dir` *was* the run's tree) and the realization of **ADR-035's branch-ownership invariant extended to tree-ownership**. Teardown is reclamation-by-lazy-sweep at the next provision: `sweepTerminalWorktrees` (run at provision start, before the new worktree is added) enumerates `git worktree list`, and for each `run-<root>` under `fishhawk-worktrees/` reads the full lineage-root run id from a `run-<root>.runid` sidecar (written at provision beside the worktree, since the short dir name can't be resolved back to a run id) and asks the backend whether that lineage is terminal via the additive `lineage_complete` field on `GET /v0/runs/{id}` (`upload.Client.RunLineageComplete`). `lineage_complete` is true when the lineage-root run AND every decomposed child are terminal (computed in `backend/internal/server/reads.go` from the `decomposed_from` child graph). **fishhawkd is the authority on lineage terminality only** (it can't reach the operator's filesystem); the runner performs the physical `git worktree remove --force` host-side. So a worktree for a terminal lineage lingers until the next provision on that host — acceptable: it lives under `.git`, is invisible to `git status`, and is cheap. The sweep is entirely best-effort (a missing sidecar, backend error, or git error logs a degradation event and is skipped; it never fails the stage and never removes a worktree it can't prove reclaimable). Relocating the run's commit off the operator checkout's HEAD broke the MCP server's `diff_summary` (which shelled `git show --numstat HEAD` in `working_dir`); the numstat totals now ride on the `git_diff` wire event (`insertions`/`deletions`) and `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_stage.go` reads them from there, keeping the MCP server worktree- and decomposition-unaware. **Parallel children (E24.4 / #1144).** A default-off runner flag `--parallel-isolate` (`runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/flags.go`) inverts the child keying: when set, `lineageRoot(runID, decomposedFromRunID, parallelIsolate)` returns the **child's own** id, so each concurrent sibling provisions an **isolated** `run-<child>` worktree (its own lock + `.runid` sidecar) instead of racing the one shared `run-<parent>` tree — the correct shape now that each child owns a distinct sole-writer slice branch (E24.1). The serial/solo paths are byte-for-byte unchanged when the flag is off. The MCP tool `fishhawk_run_children` (`backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_children.go`) is the driver that sets it: it reads the parent's `plan_decomposed` audit entry (`child_run_ids` + `effective_max_parallel`, 0 = unlimited), spawns each **pending** child's implement stage concurrently — `--parallel-isolate` appended — under an `errgroup` bounded by the orchestrator-resolved effective cap (clamp-DOWN-only against an optional `max_parallel` override), and **awaits ALL with no sibling-cancel** (a child failure is data in the consolidated per-child result, never a tool error; re-invocation is idempotent — only `pending` children are dispatched). The spawn/parse/SIGKILL-group core is the single `spawnRunnerStage` helper shared with `fishhawk_run_stage`. Per-child SIGKILL crash-survival is inherited from the same stale-lock-reclaim + lazy-sweep path (a child has no children, so its own terminality == `lineage_complete`).
373:| Decomposition concurrency cap (resolution; `effective_max_parallel`) | Decision core: `backend/internal/budget/parallel.go` (E24.6 / #1146) — pure `ParallelDecision(requested, cap) ParallelResult` with `0 = unlimited` semantics (cap≤0 → no throttle; cap<requested → `Allowed=cap, Capped=true`), the CONTRACT the E24.3 (#1143) dispatch throttle consumes — `orchestrator.go::DispatchDecomposedChildren` calls `ParallelDecision(len(pending)+len(in-flight), cap)` and dispatches `Allowed - in-flight` headroom children. Resolver: `spec.Workflow.EffectiveMaxParallel(globalDefault)` — the per-workflow `decomposition.max_parallel` knob (`backend/internal/spec/spec.go`, `Workflow.Decomposition`) wins when `>0`, else the global default; `0 = unlimited`, kept consistent with `ParallelDecision`. Global default: `server.Config.MaxParallelChildren`, wired from `FISHHAWKD_MAX_PARALLEL_CHILDREN` (flag `--max-parallel-children`, default `0`) in `serve.go` onto both the Config and `orchestrator.Orchestrator.MaxParallelChildren`. Schema: `workflow.decomposition.max_parallel` (version `0.6`, additive). Resolution + surfacing live in `orchestrator.go::fanoutIfDecomposed` → `resolveEffectiveMaxParallel` (parse the run's cached `workflow_spec`, look up the workflow, resolve the cap; best-effort — absent/unparseable spec or workflow-not-in-spec degrades to the global default with a WARN): the resolved value is logged and recorded as `effective_max_parallel` in the `plan_decomposed` audit payload. **Mechanism vs. enforcement:** #1146 RESOLVES and SURFACES the cap; E24.3 (#1143) ENFORCES it — `DispatchDecomposedChildren` dispatches pending children (ascending `slice_index`) up to the cap via the runner-kind-aware `Advance` path, wired at fanout (initial), event-driven refill in `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent`, and the `childcompletion` sweeper backstop (see the Stage-orchestrator row). |
416:| Stage orchestrator (next-stage dispatch after approve) | `backend/internal/orchestrator/`; called from approval handler on approve. Dispatches the next pending stage (or transitions Run to terminal when all stages are done). Agent stages fire `workflow_dispatch`; human stages walk to `awaiting_approval` directly. **Auto-merge stages (#255 / ADR-017)**: review stages with a check-only gate (`gate.Kind == 'check'`) take a third path — `dispatchAutoMergeStage` calls `githubclient.EnableAutoMerge` (REST GET `/pulls/{n}` for the node id, then GraphQL `enablePullRequestAutoMerge` mutation, default `SQUASH`) against the run's `pull_request_url` and walks the stage straight to `succeeded`. Fishhawk's role is "queue and step out of the way"; GitHub's auto-merge machinery handles the actual merge once branch protection clears. Failure to enable (auto-merge disabled on the repo, etc.) leaves the stage in `dispatched` and surfaces the error so a fresh `Advance` call retries — same idempotency posture as `workflow_dispatch`. **Decomposition fanout (#455 / ADR-025 D4)**: when the next pending stage is `implement` and the approved plan declares `decomposition.sub_plans`, the orchestrator mints one child run per sub-plan (carrying `parent_run_id = parent.id` + `decomposed_from = parent.id` + an issue_context derived from the sub-plan's `scope_hint`), parks the parent's implement stage in the new `awaiting_children` state, and emits a `plan_decomposed` audit entry naming the child IDs. Children themselves skip the fanout check (a non-nil `decomposed_from` short-circuits the path), so recursion is bounded at one level. **Existing-children idempotency guard (#1063)**: before minting, `fanoutIfDecomposed` probes `ListRuns(DecomposedFrom==parent, Limit:1)`; if the parent already has children (a fix-up re-open or a sweeper double-advance), it skips the fan-out and returns `(false, nil)` so `Advance` re-dispatches the parent implement stage against the existing shared branch instead of re-minting — only the first fan-out (zero children) mints. Requires both `Artifacts` and `Audit` dependencies on the Orchestrator struct; with either nil the fanout is silently disabled and the implement stage dispatches as today. **Concurrent child dispatch (E24.3 / ADR-041 / #1143)**: `DispatchDecomposedChildren` dispatches a parked parent's pending children — instead of leaving them for serial operator drive — up to the resolved concurrency cap. It lists ALL children (`listAllDecomposedChildren`), partitions them pending / in-flight / terminal, resolves the cap (`resolveEffectiveMaxParallel`), consumes `budget.ParallelDecision(pending+in-flight, cap)`, and dispatches `Allowed - in-flight` headroom children in ascending `slice_index` order via the existing runner-kind-aware `Advance` (per-backend dispatch mechanics stay owned by E24.4 local / E24.5 Actions). Each dispatched child records a `children_dispatch` `run_auto_advanced` entry via the nil-safe `Drive` engine (see the Drive-mode row). It is wired at THREE points, all best-effort (a dispatch error never unwinds the parked parent): inline at the end of `fanoutIfDecomposed` (initial dispatch), event-driven in `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent` (refill — as in-flight children settle, the next pending ones dispatch to hold the active count at the cap), and the `childcompletion` sweeper's `resolveParent` not-all-terminal branch (the fail-closed backstop, via the nil-safe `ChildDispatcher` interface so `childcompletion` stays orchestrator-free). Idempotent + soft-cap: in-flight children are counted from current state so re-entrant/overlapping calls bound to the cap and `Advance` same-state transitions no-op; a benign one-slot overshoot in a tight race never strands or double-runs a child. Decomposed children are routed onto a shared branch (`fishhawk/run-<shortParentID>`) by the runner and CLI via the `decomposed_from_run_id` field on the prompt response — see the Pull-request artifact upload chain row for the branch-sharing protocol. **Actions decomposed-child dispatch (E24.5 / #1145)**: for the `github_actions` backend the concurrent dispatch above is realized through `fireDispatch` — each child auto-advances and fires its OWN `workflow_dispatch` carrying its own `run_id`/`stage_id` against the base ref (`o.DefaultRef`, fallback `main`), bounded by the same `DispatchDecomposedChildren` cap. The runner — NOT the dispatch — derives the sole-writer slice branch `fishhawk/run-<parent>/slice-<idx>` by fetching `decomposed_from` + `slice_index` from the stage-details endpoint keyed by `run_id`; because each child's `run_id`/`stage_id` are distinct, the per-slice checkouts push to distinct slice-branch names that cannot collide. NO new `workflow_dispatch` input is added — GitHub rejects inputs not declared in the customer-side `.github/workflows/fishhawk.yml` with a 422 "Unexpected inputs provided", and the existing `run_id`/`stage_id` inputs already suffice; the dispatch carries structured `slice_index`/`decomposed_from` log fields for observability only. The customer-side `fishhawk.yml` `concurrency:` group (a `.github/workflows/**` runner-capacity guard that bounds the customer's Actions runner pool) is a separate, human-led (`autonomy:low`) change tracked as an operator-filed follow-up, OUT OF SCOPE here. **Park-on-recoverable (#698 / #1081)**: the event-driven parent-resolution hook `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent` (fired from `completeRun` on every child terminal transition) classifies each failed child's implement-stage failure via `run.ImplementFailureRecoverable` (which wraps the new `run.RecoverableInDecomposition` = `RetryableFailure || category B`). When children failed but EVERY failed child's failure is recoverable in decomposition (category A/C, a D SLA timeout, or category B via the in-place recover path below), it leaves the parent parked in `awaiting_children` instead of resolving it to `failed-C`, and emits a one-time `parent_awaiting_redrive` audit (system actor, payload `{parent_stage_id, retryable_child_run_ids}`) so an operator can re-drive the recoverable child without racing the resolution. The auto-retry / `retry_stage` path is UNCHANGED — it still consults `run.RetryableFailure` directly and keeps refusing B; only this parent-park gate broadened to B (#1081). A genuinely non-recoverable failed child — a D-rejection (approver reject), or a child whose stages can't be listed or whose implement stage carries no category — resolves the parent to `failed-C` (park only when every failure is positively confirmed recoverable, so an unclassifiable child resolves rather than parking indefinitely). **Consolidated PR on settle (#714 / ADR-032)**: in `Advance`, when the next pending stage is `review`, the run is a decomposed parent (`decomposed_from == nil` AND it has decomposed children), and `pull_request_url` is empty, `maybeOpenConsolidatedPR` opens the ONE consolidated PR for the whole decomposition BEFORE dispatching review — head = the consolidated branch `fishhawk/run-<first8(parentID)>-consolidated` (the `consolidatedBranch` helper; a NON-NESTING sibling of the slice branches, renamed under #1243 — see the fan-in row for the D/F-conflict rationale), base = `o.DefaultRef` (fallback `main`; NOT `TriggerRef`, which is an `issue:NNN` string), title/body from the run's `issue_context`. It stamps `pull_request_url` via `SetRunPullRequestURL` so the existing merge reconciler (above) resolves the review on the consolidated PR's MERGE — ADR-031's verified-landing invariant holds (the parent reaches `succeeded` only on merge, never at PR-open). Idempotency is load-bearing because the periodic sweeper and the event-driven `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent` both finish by calling `Advance`: an empty-URL re-read shrinks the double-open window, and a `githubclient.ErrPullRequestExists` (422-duplicate) recovers the already-open PR's URL via `ListOpenPullRequestsByHead` rather than failing the settle. Emits a best-effort `consolidated_pr_opened` audit (system actor). Graceful-skip (parent stays PR-less, same posture as `fireDispatch`) when the run has zero children, `o.GitHub == nil`, or `installation_id` is nil — narrowing rather than regressing prior behavior. The consolidated PR's head branch is now PRODUCED by the fan-in step below (under E24.1/#1141 each child pushes only its own slice branch and nobody creates the consolidated branch). **Fan-in integration (ADR-041 / E24.2 / #1142)**: `integrateSlices` runs on the all-children-SUCCEEDED settle path — invoked from BOTH `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent` (event-driven) and `childcompletion.resolveParent` (sweeper) BEFORE the `awaiting_children` stage is stamped succeeded — and sequentially merges each succeeded slice branch `fishhawk/run-<shortParent>/slice-<n>` (the `sliceBranch` helper, kept in sync with the runner's `childSliceBranch`) onto the consolidated branch `fishhawk/run-<shortParent>-consolidated` in ascending `slice_index` order via server-side git merges. **The consolidated branch is the NON-NESTING `-consolidated` sibling of the slice branches, NOT `fishhawk/run-<shortParent>` (#1243)**: git stores refs as a filesystem-like hierarchy under `.git/refs/heads`, so a ref whose full path (`refs/heads/fishhawk/run-<short>`) is a strict prefix of an existing slice ref's path (`refs/heads/fishhawk/run-<short>/slice-0`) cannot be created — the directory/file (D/F) conflict that 422'd `CreateRef` (and thus fan-in) 100% in production. `runBranchPrefix(id)` is the shared `fishhawk/run-<short>` namespace; `sliceBranch` nests under it (byte-identical to the runner, which is UNCHANGED) while `consolidatedBranch` appends `-consolidated` so the two never nest. The fan-in is backed by three REST primitives — `githubclient.GetBranchSHA` / `CreateRef` / `MergeBranch` (GET `/git/ref/heads/{branch}`, POST `/git/refs`, POST `/merges`). It resolves the base sha from `o.DefaultRef` (fallback `main`), creates the consolidated branch from it when absent (`CreateRef`'s 422 "already exists" is a benign idempotent no-op), then merges each slice (a `204`/already-contained is an idempotent no-op so a re-entrant settle is clean). A `409` merge conflict (`githubclient.ErrMergeConflict`) returns a STRUCTURED `*SliceConflict` (conflicting slice index + child run id); the settle path then fails the parent implement (`awaiting_children`) stage **category-B RECOVERABLE** with a stable `slice integration conflict: …` reason prefix and emits a `slice_integration_conflict` audit whose payload carries `conflicting_slice_index` + `conflicting_child_run_id` — the machine resume target `next_actions` reads back (never parsed from the reason string). A clean fan-in emits `slices_integrated` (payload `{child_run_ids, consolidated_branch, slice_count}`, consumed by E24.7) and falls through to the succeeded transition + `Advance`, which opens the consolidated PR off the now-integrated branch. The decomposed-children listing **paginates to completion** (`listAllDecomposedChildren`, `integrateSlicesPageSize`) so a fan-out exceeding one page can never silently integrate only the first page. A non-conflict GitHub error leaves the stage parked (the next tick/retry re-enters; merges are idempotent). Graceful-skip (same posture as `maybeOpenConsolidatedPR`) when `o.GitHub == nil`, `installation_id` is nil, or there are zero succeeded slices. `IntegrateSlices(ctx, parentRunID)` is the exported wrapper the sweeper's adapter calls. **Startup run-completion recovery (#727)**: `ReconcileStuckRuns(ctx)` is a one-shot self-heal called from `serve.go` at boot (gated only on `Orchestrator != nil && RunRepo != nil`, best-effort/non-fatal). It pages `ListRuns(State=running)` and, for any run whose stages are ALL terminal (`StageState.IsTerminal()`) but is itself non-terminal, calls `Advance` → `completeRun` to resolve it to `succeeded`/`failed`/`cancelled`. Skips any run with a non-terminal stage so a genuinely in-flight run is never force-completed; idempotent (an already-terminal run is a `completeRun` no-op, a re-run finds nothing). Reuses existing repo methods only (no new query) — the recovery for the `{all stages terminal, run non-terminal}` class the merge-resolution bug above produced. |
418:| Operator fan-in trigger (E24.2 / ADR-041 / #1238) | `backend/internal/server/consolidate.go::handleConsolidateRun` (`POST /v0/runs/{run_id}/consolidate`; MCP verb `fishhawk_consolidate_slices`, `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/tools.go`). The operator-drivable, error-SURFACING fan-in for a decomposed parent parked in `awaiting_children`. The child-completion sweeper (row above) is the automatic backstop, but it is off by default in the local dev `fishhawkd` ("dev-loop posture") — so `scripts/dev up` now passes `--enable-child-completion-sweeper`, and this endpoint is the explicit operator path to run (or diagnose) the fan-in. It composes the EXPORTED orchestrator primitives `IntegrateSlices` → `TransitionStage` → `Advance`, mirroring `childcompletion.resolveParent`'s all-succeeded arm, WITHOUT touching the hot event-driven/sweeper paths. The `children_settled` and `slice_integration_conflict` audit payloads are byte-identical to the sweeper's so the `children_status` integration-phase classifier reports correctly whichever path settled the parent. Where the event-driven `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent` WARN-swallows a non-conflict `IntegrateSlices` error (leaving a silent stuck parent), this returns it (`502 slice_integration_error`, stage left `awaiting_children` for retry). Outcomes (200): `integrated` (parent implement `succeeded`, consolidated PR opened — `consolidated_branch` + `pull_request_url`) or `slice_conflict` (parent implement `failed` category-B — `conflicting_slice_index` + `conflicting_child_run_id`). Preconditions, each a refusal: decomposed parent (`not_a_decomposed_parent`, 400), implement stage `awaiting_children` (`not_awaiting_children`, 409), every child terminal (`children_in_flight`, 409) and succeeded (`children_failed`, 409). Auth: `write:runs`; a run-bound MCP token is refused `403 agent_token_forbidden` (consolidation is an operator action). |

tool_use_result.stdout:
59:   - **Per-run working-tree isolation (E22.X / #1137, local loop only).** Immediately after the prompt fetch resolves `decomposed_from_run_id` — the first point the runner learns its lineage — `runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/worktree.go` provisions a **lineage-keyed git worktree** and relocates `cfg.workingDir` into it, so concurrent runs driven on one local host no longer share a single working tree (the hosted GitHub Actions path is already per-job-isolated by `actions/checkout`). The worktree is a detached checkout of HEAD at `<git-common-dir>/fishhawk-worktrees/run-<root>`, where `root` is `lineageRoot(runID, decomposedFromRunID)` = the **parent** run id for a decomposed child (so all siblings share one lineage worktree) or the run's **own** id for a solo run (its own worktree). Under ADR-041 (E24.1 / #1141) each child no longer layers its commit onto a single shared `fishhawk/run-<parent>` branch; instead each cuts and pushes its **own sole-writer slice branch** `fishhawk/run-<parent>/slice-<n>` (n = the child's `slice_index`, the sub_plan position). The shared lineage worktree sequentially checks out each distinct slice branch in turn — E24.1 retains **serial** child dispatch (the lineage lockfile serializes same-lineage stages, so git never sees one branch checked out in two worktrees at once; parallel dispatch is E24.3+). Fan-in of the slice branches onto the consolidated branch is ADR-041 / E24.2, out of scope here. The common gitdir is resolved via `git rev-parse --git-common-dir` (not `--git-dir`) so an operator checkout that is itself a linked worktree still lands under the one shared root. Because every downstream git op re-derives `repoDir := cfg.workingDir` (the FreshFetchBase / checkoutChildBase / commit / verify-gate sequences), relocating that single field isolates the whole stage with no per-block change. An O_EXCL lockfile beside the worktree (`run-<root>.lock`) serializes same-lineage stages and **fails loud** (category-A) if a live process already holds it — decomposition drives a lineage's stages sequentially, so a concurrent same-lineage stage is a corruption hazard, not a legitimate state; a lock left by a crashed prior stage (pid no longer alive) is reclaimed. The worktree lives under `.git`, invisible to the operator's `git status`. This is the **`working_dir`-means-repo-root-to-derive-from** semantic shift (previously `working_dir` *was* the run's tree) and the realization of **ADR-035's branch-ownership invariant extended to tree-ownership**. Teardown is reclamation-by-lazy-sweep at the next provision: `sweepTerminalWorktrees` (run at provision start, before the new worktree is added) enumerates `git worktree list`, and for each `run-<root>` under `fishhawk-worktrees/` reads the full lineage-root run id from a `run-<root>.runid` sidecar (written at provision beside the worktree, since the short dir name can't be resolved back to a run id) and asks the backend whether that lineage is terminal via the additive `lineage_complete` field on `GET /v0/runs/{id}` (`upload.Client.RunLineageComplete`). `lineage_complete` is true when the lineage-root run AND every decomposed child are terminal (computed in `backend/internal/server/reads.go` from the `decomposed_from` child graph). **fishhawkd is the authority on lineage terminality only** (it can't reach the operator's filesystem); the runner performs the physical `git worktree remove --force` host-side. So a worktree for a terminal lineage lingers until the next provision on that host — acceptable: it lives under `.git`, is invisible to `git status`, and is cheap. The sweep is entirely best-effort (a missing sidecar, backend error, or git error logs a degradation event and is skipped; it never fails the stage and never removes a worktree it can't prove reclaimable). Relocating the run's commit off the operator checkout's HEAD broke the MCP server's `diff_summary` (which shelled `git show --numstat HEAD` in `working_dir`); the numstat totals now ride on the `git_diff` wire event (`insertions`/`deletions`) and `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_stage.go` reads them from there, keeping the MCP server worktree- and decomposition-unaware. **Parallel children (E24.4 / #1144).** A default-off runner flag `--parallel-isolate` (`runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/flags.go`) inverts the child keying: when set, `lineageRoot(runID, decomposedFromRunID, parallelIsolate)` returns the **child's own** id, so each concurrent sibling provisions an **isolated** `run-<child>` worktree (its own lock + `.runid` sidecar) instead of racing the one shared `run-<parent>` tree — the correct shape now that each child owns a distinct sole-writer slice branch (E24.1). The serial/solo paths are byte-for-byte unchanged when the flag is off. The MCP tool `fishhawk_run_children` (`backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_children.go`) is the driver that sets it: it reads the parent's `plan_decomposed` audit entry (`child_run_ids` + `effective_max_parallel`, 0 = unlimited), spawns each **pending** child's implement stage concurrently — `--parallel-isolate` appended — under an `errgroup` bounded by the orchestrator-resolved effective cap (clamp-DOWN-only against an optional `max_parallel` override), and **awaits ALL with no sibling-cancel** (a child failure is data in the consolidated per-child result, never a tool error; re-invocation is idempotent — only `pending` children are dispatched). The spawn/parse/SIGKILL-group core is the single `spawnRunnerStage` helper shared with `fishhawk_run_stage`. Per-child SIGKILL crash-survival is inherited from the same stale-lock-reclaim + lazy-sweep path (a child has no children, so its own terminality == `lineage_complete`).
373:| Decomposition concurrency cap (resolution; `effective_max_parallel`) | Decision core: `backend/internal/budget/parallel.go` (E24.6 / #1146) — pure `ParallelDecision(requested, cap) ParallelResult` with `0 = unlimited` semantics (cap≤0 → no throttle; cap<requested → `Allowed=cap, Capped=true`), the CONTRACT the E24.3 (#1143) dispatch throttle consumes — `orchestrator.go::DispatchDecomposedChildren` calls `ParallelDecision(len(pending)+len(in-flight), cap)` and dispatches `Allowed - in-flight` headroom children. Resolver: `spec.Workflow.EffectiveMaxParallel(globalDefault)` — the per-workflow `decomposition.max_parallel` knob (`backend/internal/spec/spec.go`, `Workflow.Decomposition`) wins when `>0`, else the global default; `0 = unlimited`, kept consistent with `ParallelDecision`. Global default: `server.Config.MaxParallelChildren`, wired from `FISHHAWKD_MAX_PARALLEL_CHILDREN` (flag `--max-parallel-children`, default `0`) in `serve.go` onto both the Config and `orchestrator.Orchestrator.MaxParallelChildren`. Schema: `workflow.decomposition.max_parallel` (version `0.6`, additive). Resolution + surfacing live in `orchestrator.go::fanoutIfDecomposed` → `resolveEffectiveMaxParallel` (parse the run's cached `workflow_spec`, look up the workflow, resolve the cap; best-effort — absent/unparseable spec or workflow-not-in-spec degrades to the global default with a WARN): the resolved value is logged and recorded as `effective_max_parallel` in the `plan_decomposed` audit payload. **Mechanism vs. enforcement:** #1146 RESOLVES and SURFACES the cap; E24.3 (#1143) ENFORCES it — `DispatchDecomposedChildren` dispatches pending children (ascending `slice_index`) up to the cap via the runner-kind-aware `Advance` path, wired at fanout (initial), event-driven refill in `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent`, and the `childcompletion` sweeper backstop (see the Stage-orchestrator row). |

The consolidated PR's head branch is now PRODUCED by the fan-in step below (under E24.1/#1141 each child pushes only its own slice branch and nobody creates the consolidated branch). **Fan-in integration (ADR-041 / E24.2 / #1142)**: `integrateSlices` runs on the all-children-SUCCEEDED settle path — invoked from BOTH `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent` (event-driven) and `childcompletion.resolveParent` (sweeper) BEFORE the `awaiting_children` stage is stamped succeeded — and sequentially merges each succeeded slice branch `fishhawk/run-<shortParent>/slice-<n>` (the `sliceBranch` helper, kept in sync with the runner's `childSliceBranch`) onto the consolidated branch `fishhawk/run-<shortParent>-consolidated` in ascending `slice_index` order via server-side git merges. **The consolidated branch is the NON-NESTING `-consolidated` sibling of the slice branches, NOT `fishhawk/run-<shortParent>` (#1243)**: git stores refs as a filesystem-like hierarchy under `.git/refs/heads`, so a ref whose full path (`refs/heads/fishhawk/run-<short>`) is a strict prefix of an existing slice ref's path (`refs/heads/fishhawk/run-<short>/slice-0`) cannot be created — the directory/file (D/F) conflict that 422'd `CreateRef` (and thus fan-in) 100% in production. `runBranchPrefix(id)` is the shared `fishhawk/run-<short>` namespace; `sliceBranch` nests under it (byte-identical to the runner, which is UNCHANGED) while `consolidatedBranch` appends `-consolidated` so the two never nest. The fan-in is backed by three REST primitives — `githubclient.GetBranchSHA` / `CreateRef` / `MergeBranch` (GET `/git/ref/heads/{branch}`, POST `/git/refs`, POST `/merges`). It resolves the base sha from `o.DefaultRef` (fallback `main`), creates the consolidated branch from it when absent (`CreateRef`'s 422 "already exists" is a benign idempotent no-op), then merges each slice (a `204`/already-contained is an idempotent no-op so a re-entrant settle is clean). A `409` merge conflict (`githubclient.ErrMergeConflict`) returns a STRUCTURED `*SliceConflict` (conflicting slice index + child run id); the settle path then fails the parent implement (`awaiting_children`) stage **category-B RECOVERABLE** with a stable `slice integration conflict: …` reason prefix and emits a `slice_integration_conflict` audit whose payload carries `conflicting_slice_index` + `conflicting_child_run_id` — the machine resume target `next_actions` reads back (never parsed from the reason string). A clean fan-in emits `slices_integrated` (payload `{child_run_ids, consolidated_branch, slice_count}`, consumed by E24.7) and falls through to the succeeded transition + `Advance`, which opens the consolidated PR off the now-integrated branch. The decomposed-children listing **paginates to completion** (`listAllDecomposedChildren`, `integrateSlicesPageSize`) so a fan-out exceeding one page can never silently integrate only the first page. A non-conflict GitHub error leaves the stage parked (the next tick/retry re-enters; merges are idempotent). Graceful-skip (same posture as `maybeOpenConsolidatedPR`) when `o.GitHub == nil`, `installation_id` is nil, or there are zero succeeded slices. `IntegrateSlices(ctx, parentRunID)` is the exported wrapper the sweeper's adapter calls. **Startup run-completion recovery (#727)**: `ReconcileStuckRuns(ctx)` is a one-shot self-heal called from `serve.go` at boot (gated only on `Orchestrator != nil && RunRepo != nil`, best-effort/non-fatal). It pages `ListRuns(State=running)` and, for any run whose stages are ALL terminal (`StageState.IsTerminal()`) but is itself non-terminal, calls `Advance` → `completeRun` to resolve it to `succeeded`/`failed`/`cancelled`. Skips any run with a non-terminal stage so a genuinely in-flight run is never force-completed; idempotent (an already-terminal run is a `completeRun` no-op, a re-run finds nothing). Reuses existing repo methods only (no new query) — the recovery for the `{all stages terminal, run non-terminal}` class the merge-resolution bug above produced. |
418:| Operator fan-in trigger (E24.2 / ADR-041 / #1238) | `backend/internal/server/consolidate.go::handleConsolidateRun` (`POST /v0/runs/{run_id}/consolidate`; MCP verb `fishhawk_consolidate_slices`, `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/tools.go`). The operator-drivable, error-SURFACING fan-in for a decomposed parent parked in `awaiting_children`. The child-completion sweeper (row above) is the automatic backstop, but it is off by default in the local dev `fishhawkd` ("dev-loop posture") — so `scripts/dev up` now passes `--enable-child-completion-sweeper`, and this endpoint is the explicit operator path to run (or diagnose) the fan-in. It composes the EXPORTED orchestrator primitives `IntegrateSlices` → `TransitionStage` → `Advance`, mirroring `childcompletion.resolveParent`'s all-succeeded arm, WITHOUT touching the hot event-driven/sweeper paths. The `children_settled` and `slice_integration_conflict` audit payloads are byte-identical to the sweeper's so the `children_status` integration-phase classifier reports correctly whichever path settled the parent. Where the event-driven `maybeAdvanceDecomposedParent` WARN-swallows a non-conflict `IntegrateSlices` error (leaving a silent stuck parent), this returns it (`502 slice_integration_error`, stage left `awaiting_children` for retry). Outcomes (200): `integrated` (parent implement `succeeded`, consolidated PR opened — `consolidated_branch` + `pull_request_url`) or `slice_conflict` (parent implement `failed` category-B — `conflicting_slice_index` + `conflicting_child_run_id`). Preconditions, each a refusal: decomposed parent (`not_a_decomposed_parent`, 400), implement stage `awaiting_children` (`not_awaiting_children`, 409), every child terminal (`children_in_flight`, 409) and succeeded (`children_failed`, 409). Auth: `write:runs`; a run-bound MCP token is refused `403 agent_token_forbidden` (consolidation is an operator action). |

Preconditions, each a refusal: decomposed parent (`not_a_decomposed_parent`, 400), implement stage `awaiting_children` (`not_awaiting_children`, 409), every child terminal (`children_in_flight`, 409) and succeeded (`children_failed`, 409). Auth: `write:runs`; a run-bound MCP token is refused `403 agent_token_forbidden` (consolidation is an operator action). |
419	| Self-consistency invariant monitor (#764) | `backend/internal/invariantmonitor/`: `Ticker.Tick` runs two invariants per pass. **Invariant 1** ({all stages terminal, run non-terminal}) auto-reconciles via the `Reconcile` func wired to `orchestrator.ReconcileStuckRuns` — the same safe self-heal the one-shot startup path runs (#727), now periodic. **Invariant 2** ({review stage `awaiting_approval`, null/empty `pull_request_url`}) is **surface-only**: it pages `ListRuns(state=running)` + `ListStagesForRun` (no new sqlc query) and emits a `system`-actor `invariant_violation` audit entry (payload `{kind, run_id, reconciled:false}`) plus a WARN log — it mutates nothing because the missing PR is the unrecoverable fact (#742). Invariant 2 fires **only** for runs that genuinely intended to open a PR: `runIntendsPR` parses the run's cached `WorkflowSpec` and flags only when a stage in the run's workflow produces a `pull_request` artifact, so non-PR (commit-yourself) workflows — whose null PR is the legitimate normal state — are never flagged (a run whose intent can't be determined is left silent). Off by default; enable with `--enable-invariant-monitor` (`FISHHAWKD_ENABLE_INVARIANT_MONITOR=true`), scan interval `--invariant-monitor-interval` (default 60s). Mirrors the `dispatchwatchdog.Ticker` shape; per-run errors are best-effort/logged and never abort the sweep. The durable audit entry + structured WARN are the `metric` surface for now — a real prometheus/expvar exporter is deferred. |
420	| DB-unavailable auth classification (503 vs 401, #764) | `backend/internal/dberr/`: `IsUnavailable(err)` classifies a database-UNAVAILABLE condition (connection refused → `*pgconn.ConnectError`, closed pool → puddle/v2 `ErrClosedPool`, `net.OpError` fallback) apart from an ordinary query outcome (no rows / `ErrNotFound`). The auth middleware `Server.bearerAuth` (`backend/internal/server/middleware.go`) captures the error from each authenticator (apitoken / mcptoken / session) and, when `dberr.IsUnavailable` is true for the credential presented, short-circuits with `503 service_unavailable` (`database unavailable; retry shortly`) instead of masking the outage as a fall-through 401. A genuinely bad credential on a HEALTHY DB (`ErrNotFound`) is NOT unavailable, so it still falls through to the anonymous identity and the per-handler 401 is unchanged — this is an error-CLASSIFICATION change, not an authorization tightening (no scope/role/audience check added). |

input.new_string:
| Topological-wave dispatch (E24.X / #1278 slice B) | **plan_decomposed `waves`**: `orchestrator.go::fanoutIfDecomposed` now computes `waves [][]int` via `plan.Waves(decomposition)` (slice A, #1280 — a pure Kahn topological sort of the sub_plans' `depends_on` edges) and threads it through `emitPlanDecomposed` into the `plan_decomposed` audit payload alongside `child_run_ids`/`effective_max_parallel`. The waves carry SLICE INDICES that positionally index `child_run_ids` (`child_run_ids[i]` is slice `i`). A should-be-impossible `Waves` error (the plan was already validated in slice A) falls back to a single all-indices wave (`singleAllIndicesWave`) with a WARN. `waves` is additive (the audit map always carries it; the MCP `PlanDecomposed.Waves` decodes it `omitempty`, nil → single wave). **Non-settling per-wave fan-in**: `backend/internal/server/consolidate.go::handleIntegrateWave` (`POST /v0/runs/{run_id}/integrate-wave`; client `apiClient.IntegrateWave`). It reuses the EXACT same exported `IntegrateSlices` primitive `/consolidate` uses (no new git-merge code) to merge the slices SUCCEEDED SO FAR onto the consolidated branch, but — UNLIKE `/consolidate` — does NOT require all children terminal, does NOT transition the parent stage, and does NOT advance/open the PR (the parent stage is identical before/after on BOTH the `integrated` and `slice_conflict` outcomes; the terminal fan-in stays `/consolidate`'s job after the last wave). It shares `/consolidate`'s auth + decomposed-parent precondition posture (`agent_token_forbidden`/`insufficient_scope` 403, `not_a_decomposed_parent` 400, `slice_integration_error` 502) minus the terminal-children gates. **run_children wave loop**: `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_children.go` replaces the single global errgroup with an ordered per-wave loop — each wave's pending children dispatch concurrently under the cap against `currentBase` (passed as both `--base-branch` and `--check-base-ref` so wave N cuts from the prior wave's merged tree), then between waves it calls `IntegrateWave` and re-bases the next wave on the returned `consolidated_branch`. A nil/empty `waves` (old entry or no-depends_on decomposition) collapses to a single all-indices wave dispatched against `main` and NEVER calls integrate-wave (byte-for-byte the pre-#1278 behavior). Guards (each surfaced as a warning, loop stops): a dispatched wave-N child that did not succeed (no partial-wave integration), a `slice_conflict` or transport error from integrate-wave, and an empty `consolidated_branch` (GitHub-not-wired graceful-skip — keeps `currentBase` unchanged rather than dispatching against an empty ref). A waves index out of range is a loud tool error. |
| Self-consistency invariant monitor (#764) |

tool_use_result.originalFile:
# Fishhawk — Architecture

> **Status:** Living document; tracks the current technical realization of the v0 spec.
> **Audience:** Agents and engineers working in this repo. Optimized for density, not narrative.
> **Authority:** `docs/MVP_SPEC.md` defines scope; this doc defines the *technical realization* of that scope. When they disagree, MVP_SPEC wins for "what" and this doc wins for "how".

## 1. System summary

Fishhawk is **a governed, auditable workflow layer for agent-driven software changes**. A customer commits a YAML workflow spec to their repo, triggers it (issue label, CLI, UI), and the system runs the agent on their CI under typed constraints, captures a signed trace, and gates each stage on human approval. The product surface is the workflow execution and audit history; the agent itself is pluggable.

See `docs/MVP_SPEC.md` §1–§4 for product framing, primitives, and customer-side spec syntax.

## 2. Components

Five surfaces, deployed independently. Each maps to a directory in this monorepo and an epic in Project #7.

| Component | Path | Role | Epic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Backend control plane** (`fishhawkd`) | `/backend` | Workflow state machine, policy evaluator, approval state, audit writer, REST API, GitHub App webhook receiver. | E3 (#3) |
| **Runner action** (`fishhawk/runner`) | `/runner` | GitHub Action published as `kuhlman-labs/fishhawk/runner@runner/vX.Y.Z`. Runs on the customer's CI: invokes the agent, captures trace, validates the produced plan, signs and ships the bundle. Self-execution in this repo uses `./runner` (the local path); external customers pin a release tag. Versioned, cosign-signed releases via `.github/workflows/runner-release.yml`. | E5 (#5) |
| **Web UI** | `/frontend` | Authenticated SPA — plan review, approval, audit search, run visualization. | E7 (#7) |
| **CLI** (`fishhawk`) | `/cli` | Validate workflow specs locally (`fishhawk validate`); trigger and inspect runs from the terminal. Plan review and approval explicitly stay in the UI. | E6 (#6) |
| **GitHub App** | `docs/github-app/manifest.template.json` (registered with GitHub from this template) | Per-installation tokens for repo access; OAuth provider for user sign-in; webhook source for triggers. Render via `scripts/render-github-app-manifest.sh <backend-url>`; setup docs in `docs/github-app/README.md`. | E4 (#4) |

Plus the canonical artifact, **`.fishhawk/workflows.yaml`**, which lives in the customer's repo (and in this repo for self-execution starting Day 21).

## 3. Tech stack

Consolidated from the resolved ADRs (#65–#73, #78). Each row links to the issue with full Decision and Consequences.

| Layer | Choice | ADR |
|---|---|---|
| Backend language | Go 1.22+ | MVP_SPEC §10 #1 |
| HTTP server / router | stdlib `net/http` + Go 1.22 method-aware `ServeMux` | session decision (CLAUDE.md) |
| Logger | `log/slog` (stdlib) with JSON handler | (E3.2 #42) |
| DB driver / access | `pgx/v5` + `sqlc` codegen; queries in `*.sql`, generated Go in `internal/<feature>/db/` | #66 |
| DB migrations | `golang-migrate/migrate` v4; `.up.sql/.down.sql` files; deploy-time application via `fishhawkd migrate` subcommand. Data migrations (operations that can't be expressed in idempotent SQL) ship as `fishhawkd` subcommands — e.g. `audit-rehash` in `backend/internal/auditrehash` rewrites `entry_hash` + `prev_hash` under the canonical hash algorithm (#302). The append-only triggers on `audit_entries` get temporarily disabled inside the rehash transaction so the operation is safely scoped — rollback restores the trigger if anything fails. | #70 |
| Object storage | AWS S3 (prod) + MinIO via docker-compose (dev) | #67 |
| Trace bundle format | JSON Lines + gzip (`*.jsonl.gz`); one event per line with manifest + trailer | #71 |
| Trace signing | Ed25519 over `sha256(raw_bundle_bytes)`; per-run ephemeral keys, 30-min TTL | #72 |
| Cloud | AWS (RDS Postgres, S3, ECS Fargate, ALB, Secrets Manager, CloudWatch) | #65, #73 |
| Frontend framework | Vite 6 + React 19 + React Router 7 (SPA); pnpm 10 | session decision (CLAUDE.md) |
| Frontend test runner | Vitest 3 + @testing-library/react + jsdom | E7.1 (#37) |
| Frontend styling | Tailwind CSS v4 + shadcn/ui (copied components) + Radix primitives + lucide-react icons | #68 |
| Browser auth | HTTP-only `fishhawk_session` cookie (server-side state); CSRF token in `__Host-csrf` cookie for state-changing endpoints | #69 |
| CLI / API auth | Scoped opaque bearer tokens; revocable; audit-logged on issue/use/revoke | #69 (E4.5 #51) |
| Lint | golangci-lint v2 (curated preset: errcheck, govet, ineffassign, revive, staticcheck + gofmt, goimports) | #78 |
| CI | GitHub Actions; path-aware via `dorny/paths-filter`; loops `go.work` modules | #78 |

## 4. Workflow run lifecycle

Per `docs/MVP_SPEC.md` §5.2. Concrete realization in this codebase:

1. **Trigger** — GitHub issue label/assignment (webhook → backend), CLI `fishhawk run start`, or UI button. Backend validates the workflow spec at the issue's `.fishhawk/workflows.yaml` SHA, creates a `runs` row, and emits `workflow_dispatch` to the customer's repo invoking `fishhawk/runner@runner/vX.Y.Z`.
2. **Plan stage** — Runner checks out the repo, calls backend `POST /v0/runs/{id}/signing-key` (with GitHub OIDC token) and receives an Ed25519 private key + run metadata. Invokes Claude Code with the plan prompt. Captures full trace as JSON Lines events. Validates the plan artifact against `standard_v1` schema (E1.5 / #20). Signs `sha256(bundle)`. Ships `(bundle, sig)` to `POST /v0/runs/{id}/trace`.
3. **Backend ingest** — Verifies signature against the stored public key for `run_id`. Stores the bundle in S3 keyed by content sha256. Persists the plan artifact in `artifacts` and renders it as a comment on the originating GitHub issue (mode: `rendered_comment`, kept in sync). Transitions stage state.
4. **Plan approval** — Approver reads the plan in the Web UI (canonical surface) or the issue comment (read-only echo). Clicks Approve. Backend records `(approver_subject, surface, ts)` in the audit log and transitions to implement.
5. **Implement stage** — Runner re-invokes with the implement prompt + approved plan. Captures trace. Post-hoc evaluates constraints (`forbidden_paths`, `max_files_changed`, `required_outcomes`) against the produced diff. If a constraint hits, the stage fails as category B. Otherwise pushes branch, opens PR via the GitHub App's installation token, ships final trace.
   - **Per-run working-tree isolation (E22.X / #1137, local loop only).** Immediately after the prompt fetch resolves `decomposed_from_run_id` — the first point the runner learns its lineage — `runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/worktree.go` provisions a **lineage-keyed git worktree** and relocates `cfg.workingDir` into it, so concurrent runs driven on one local host no longer share a single working tree (the hosted GitHub Actions path is already per-job-isolated by `actions/checkout`). The worktree is a detached checkout of HEAD at `<git-common-dir>/fishhawk-worktrees/run-<root>`, where `root` is `lineageRoot(runID, decomposedFromRunID)` = the **parent** run id for a decomposed child (so all siblings share one lineage worktree) or the run's **own** id for a solo run (its own worktree). Under ADR-041 (E24.1 / #1141) each child no longer layers its commit onto a single shared `fishhawk/run-<parent>` branch; instead each cuts and pushes its **own sole-writer slice branch** `fishhawk/run-<parent>/slice-<n>` (n = the child's `slice_index`, the sub_plan position). The shared lineage worktree sequentially checks out each distinct slice branch in turn — E24.1 retains **serial** child dispatch (the lineage lockfile serializes same-lineage stages, so git never sees one branch checked out in two worktrees at once; parallel dispatch is E24.3+). Fan-in of the slice branches onto the consolidated branch is ADR-041 / E24.2, out of scope here. The common gitdir is resolved via `git rev-parse --git-common-dir` (not `--git-dir`) so an operator checkout that is itself a linked worktree still lands under the one shared root. Because every downstream git op re-derives `repoDir := cfg.workingDir` (the FreshFetchBase / checkoutChildBase / commit / verify-gate sequences), relocating that single field isolates the whole stage with no per-block change. An O_EXCL lockfile beside the worktree (`run-<root>.lock`) serializes same-lineage stages and **fails loud** (category-A) if a live process already holds it — decomposition drives a lineage's stages sequentially, so a concurrent same-lineage stage is a corruption hazard, not a legitimate state; a lock left by a crashed prior stage (pid no longer alive) is reclaimed. The worktree lives under `.git`, invisible to the operator's `git status`. This is the **`working_dir`-means-repo-root-to-derive-from** semantic shift (previously `working_dir` *was* the run's tree) and the realization of **ADR-035's branch-ownership invariant extended to tree-ownership**.
