Teardown is reclamation-by-lazy-sweep at the next provision: `sweepTerminalWorktrees` (run at provision start, before the new worktree is added) enumerates `git worktree list`, and for each `run-<root>` under `fishhawk-worktrees/` reads the full lineage-root run id from a `run-<root>.runid` sidecar (written at provision beside the worktree, since the short dir name can't be resolved back to a run id) and asks the backend whether that lineage is terminal via the additive `lineage_complete` field on `GET /v0/runs/{id}` (`upload.Client.RunLineageComplete`). `lineage_complete` is true when the lineage-root run AND every decomposed child are terminal (computed in `backend/internal/server/reads.go` from the `decomposed_from` child graph). **fishhawkd is the authority on lineage terminality only** (it can't reach the operator's filesystem); the runner performs the physical `git worktree remove --force` host-side. So a worktree for a terminal lineage lingers until the next provision on that host — acceptable: it lives under `.git`, is invisible to `git status`, and is cheap. The sweep is entirely best-effort (a missing sidecar, backend error, or git error logs a degradation event and is skipped; it never fails the stage and never removes a worktree it can't prove reclaimable). Relocating the run's commit off the operator checkout's HEAD broke the MCP server's `diff_summary` (which shelled `git show --numstat HEAD` in `working_dir`); the numstat totals now ride on the `git_diff` wire event (`insertions`/`deletions`) and `backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_stage.go` reads them from there, keeping the MCP server worktree- and decomposition-unaware. **Parallel children (E24.4 / #1144).** A default-off runner flag `--parallel-isolate` (`runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/flags.go`) inverts the child keying: when set, `lineageRoot(runID, decomposedFromRunID, parallelIsolate)` returns the **child's own** id, so each concurrent sibling provisions an **isolated** `run-<child>` worktree (its own lock + `.runid` sidecar) instead of racing the one shared `run-<parent>` tree — the correct shape now that each child owns a distinct sole-writer slice branch (E24.1). The serial/solo paths are byte-for-byte unchanged when the flag is off. The MCP tool `fishhawk_run_children` (`backend/cmd/fishhawk-mcp/run_children.go`) is the driver that sets it: it reads the parent's `plan_decomposed` audit entry (`child_run_ids` + `effective_max_parallel`, 0 = unlimited), spawns each **pending** child's implement stage concurrently — `--parallel-isolate` appended — under an `errgroup` bounded by the orchestrator-resolved effective cap (clamp-DOWN-only against an optional `max_parallel` override), and **awaits ALL with no sibling-cancel** (a child failure is data in the consolidated per-child result, never a tool error; re-invocation is idempotent — only `pending` children are dispatched). The spawn/parse/SIGKILL-group core is the single `spawnRunnerStage` helper shared with `fishhawk_run_stage`. Per-child SIGKILL crash-survival is inherited from the same stale-lock-reclaim + lazy-sweep path (a child has no children, so its own terminality == `lineage_complete`).
   - **Pre-push compile gate (#728).** On the standalone (non-decomposed) PR-opening path, after the scope-only commit is created and **before** the push, the runner (`runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/main.go::verifyCommittedTreeCompiles`, wired via `gitops.CommitAndPushArgs.VerifyCommit`) compiles the *committed* tree in an isolated `git worktree --detach` at the new HEAD SHA — not the working tree, which still carries the excluded scope-drift on disk — and runs `go vet ./...` per `go.work` module. `go vet` typechecks test files (which `go build` skips), so it catches the interface-ripple class where a scope-bounded commit drops a build-required conformance stub left in a test file (`does not implement <iface> (missing method ...)`). The block condition (#959) is **any clean compiler/typecheck diagnostic line** — `(vet: )?file.go:LINE:COL: message` (`goDiagnosticBlocks`), with the `compileDiagnosticMarkers` substring allowlist kept only as a backstop — unless every such line is a recognized dependency-resolution form (`no required module provides`, `missing go.sum entry`, `cannot find module/package`, `cannot query module`; dep failures print in the same `file.go:LINE:COL:` shape under a cold cache/offline, so the exclusion is load-bearing). The marker-allowlist-only era chased the open-ended typecheck-message tail one production bug at a time (#774, then #959's `unknown field ... in struct literal` skipping as `vet_nonzero_non_compile` and pushing an uncompilable head on run 07bce059). A real compile failure fails the stage **category B** (`gitops.ErrCommitWouldNotCompile`, re-scope/re-plan) with the drift file list, and because it runs pre-push, origin is untouched — no non-compiling PR is opened. Every infrastructure case (no drift, non-Go repo, no Go toolchain, worktree/`go work edit` failure, a nonzero exit with no parseable diagnostic, or dep-resolution-only diagnostics) is a non-blocking skip (`compile_gate_skipped`), degrading to the prior "open the PR" behavior — but a skip now requires evidence the toolchain/dependency infrastructure failed, not merely an unrecognized diagnostic message; a pure vet analyzer finding consequently blocks too (CI's golangci-lint bundles govet, and a block routes to `verify_fix_reinvoke` rather than a dead stage). Decomposed children are intentionally ungated (a child may legitimately not compile before later children land); gating the consolidated decomposition tip is a follow-up.
   - **Pre-push test gate (#800).** After the `go vet` compile check passes, the same committed-HEAD worktree runs a second phase: `go test` (no `-race` — CI runs the race build; this is the fast touched-package check) on the *touched packages* — the packages containing the declared `scope.files`, derived per `go.work` module (`touchedPackageArgs` → `./<reldir>/...` patterns), **not** the drift dirs (drift is excluded from the committed tree). This catches the drift-excluded-test-failure class (#780/#776) where a scope-only commit compiles but a necessary test fake/helper was dropped as scope drift, so the committed tree's tests are red while the agent's full working tree is green. A genuine test failure — positively identified by a `--- FAIL`/`panic:` allowlist (`looksLikeTestFailure`) — fails the stage **category B** (`gitops.ErrCommittedTestsFailed`, naming the failing package) and, running pre-push, leaves origin untouched. A `FAIL <pkg> [build failed]` **accompanied by a clean compiler diagnostic line** (`goDiagnosticBlocks`, #959) is a definitive compile failure of the tree being pushed, not infra — it blocks as `gitops.ErrCommitWouldNotCompile` (previously it skipped as `test_nonzero_non_failure`, the second half of the run-07bce059 miss). Only genuine test-infrastructure failures (`[setup failed]`, bare `[build failed]` with no diagnostic, dep resolution, exec error) skip non-blocking (`test_gate_skipped`); the skip-on-uncertainty asymmetry narrows to: uncertain means **no parseable diagnostic**, not an unrecognized diagnostic message — and a block routes to `verify_fix_reinvoke` (an in-place agent fix) rather than killing the stage, so the false-block cost that originally justified the wide skip no longer holds. The test phase runs ONLY on the drift-present path (the `len(drift)==0` and empty-scope fast paths return first), bounding cost to the drift-excluded-test-failure class; an always-run broad verify is the #651 evaluator-optimizer's job, which builds remediation on this detection foundation.
   - **Pre-push created-out-of-scope gate (#818, generalized to the open-PR path by #825).** A net-new (untracked) file outside the approved `scope.files` is silently stripped from the scope-only commit by `StageScoped` (#581) while an in-scope edit that *references* it still lands, shipping a self-inconsistent, misleadingly-green partial result. The same `gitops.CommitAndPushArgs.VerifyCommit` hook, before the compile gate, calls `gitops.UntrackedPaths` (`git ls-files --others --exclude-standard`) on the post-commit working tree to find the CREATED (untracked) subset of the scope drift; if non-empty it fails the stage **category B** (`gitops.ErrCreatedOutOfScope`, naming the files) BEFORE the push, so origin is untouched. The gate runs on the normal **open-PR implement push** and the **fix-up pass** (`cfg.fixup`, `fishhawk_fixup_stage`), but **not** on decomposed children (`cfg.fixup || (!isFixup && !isDecomposed)`) — a child may legitimately create files a later child declares. The two paths emit distinct events and wrap distinct sentinels: the open-PR path emits `created_out_of_scope`, wraps `gitops.ErrCreatedOutOfScope`, and directs the operator to amend the scope (name the files in the approval condition, or use the structured `add_scope_files` param once it exists) or replan; the fix-up path emits `fixup_created_out_of_scope`, wraps the `gitops.ErrFixupCreatedOutOfScope` specialization (which itself wraps `ErrCreatedOutOfScope`, so the single `errors.Is(err, gitops.ErrCreatedOutOfScope)` classification check matches both), and the backend's #788 fix-up recovery then restores the run to its pre-fix-up review gate so the operator hand-applies the named files or starts a fresh run with corrected scope. Only CREATED files trip this — modified-but-out-of-scope drift stays flag-only (ADR-027) — and a *legitimately-declared* new file is IN `scope.files` and therefore not in the drift set, so the gate only ever trips on net-new files OUTSIDE the declared scope. Making the fix-up tool itself succeed for a legitimately-needed new file (a fix-up-scoped allow-list) is deferred.
   - **Pre-push scope-completeness (shortfall) gate (#1151).** The inverse of the created-out-of-scope (#818/#825) and #980 commit-in-scope assertions: those prove every *committed* path is *declared*; this proves every *declared concrete* path is *committed*. The same `gitops.CommitAndPushArgs.VerifyCommit` hook, after the created-out-of-scope gate and before the compile gate, calls `gitops.MissingScopeFiles` — `git diff-tree -r -z --no-commit-id --name-only <head>` (the same NUL-separated, quotePath-immune enumeration `assertCommitInScope` uses) — to find the set of declared `scope.files` paths the commit did NOT touch, and returns the committed set alongside so the runner renders a precise `declared N scope file(s), committed M; missing: <paths>` message (each missing path annotated with its declared operation) and populates the `scope_files_missing` event's `committed` field from real data. A non-empty shortfall fails the stage **category B** (`gitops.ErrScopeFilesMissing`) BEFORE the push, so origin is untouched — catching the subset-PR class (#1148, run a4cfd41b: 8 declared scope files, only 6 committed, dropping the two edits that actually fixed the bug; PR #1149 opened anyway and burned a full review + a no-op fix-up). The gate runs on the **standalone open-PR implement push ONLY** (`!isFixup && !isDecomposed`): a fix-up legitimately touches fewer files than the full scope, and a decomposed child carries a narrowed slice scope on a shared branch. Trailing-slash directory-prefix scope entries are skipped (`newScopeMatcher`'s split semantics — a folded directory cannot require any specific touched path), and a declared `delete`-operation path the commit actually deletes shows up as a changed path so it counts as touched. **Run/stage-scoped in-band self-exempt (#1153).** A deliberately-unchanged declared scope file no longer forces a replan: the implement agent may justify it in-band by writing a JSON sidecar to the **run/stage-keyed** path `prompt.ScopeJustificationPath(run_id, stage_id)` = `/tmp/fishhawk-scope-justifications-<run_id>-<stage_id>.json` (mirrored verbatim by the runner's `scopeJustificationPath`, the same independent-module coordination as `PullRequestDescriptionPath`), listing `{path, reason}` entries inside `{run_id, stage_id, exemptions:[…]}`. After the agent settles, `run()` calls `loadScopeExemptions` (standalone open-PR path only), validates **fail-closed** (well-formed JSON, embedded `run_id`/`stage_id` matching the stage, each path concretely declared, each reason non-empty), emits a single `scope_files_exempted` trace event (folded into `gate_evidence` for the implement reviewer AND surfaced to the audit), and threads the validated set into `openPRAndShipArtifact`, where the gate subtracts the exemptions from `missing` (`partitionExemptedMissing`) before the `len>0` check — an all-exempted shortfall passes, a partial one still fails listing only the unexempted remainder. Three independent freshness defenses stop a stale sidecar from a different run exempting this run's missing file: the path is **run/stage-keyed**, the embedded `run_id`/`stage_id` are **validated** against the stage, and the keyed path is **deleted pre-invoke** (`sweepStaleScopeJustification`) and on every `loadScopeExemptions` return (consumed-cleanup). Every malformed/stale/empty-reason/undeclared case yields ZERO exemptions, so the strict gate is preserved — a defect can only ever make the gate MORE strict, never bypass it. The self-exempt is inert for fix-ups and decomposed children (the gate is already excluded there, and the prompt section is gated on `ScopeConstraint == nil`). **Presence != correctness (#1169).** This is a PRESENCE check — it proves a declared path was *touched*, not that the required change was *made*, so a comment-only / no-op edit to a declared file passes (run 5aaf89fa: an explanatory comment was added to the adr numbering block instead of wiring the value, and the gate passed). The robust gate for conventions / config / default-value changes is a done-means **behavioral** test that asserts the shipped behavior and runs in the committed-tree verify (`gitops.ErrCommittedTestsFailed`) — now required by the plan-prompt "Done-means test rule" (`backend/internal/prompt`), which closes the gap from the planning end — and its sibling plan-prompt "Per-failure-mode test rule" (#1199) extends this from one behavioral test per change to one assertion per enumerated failure / fail-closed mode, paired with an always-on implement-prompt per-failure-mode test checklist; the agent review remains the backstop. The diff-size / content-shape heuristic (the issue's option 2) was considered and rejected as low-confidence and false-positive-prone per #1169; `gitops.MissingScopeFiles` stays a presence check by design.
   - **Committed-tree verify-fix loop (#651).** When `executor.verify.max_iterations > 0`, the implement push path (`stageType == "implement" && !cfg.noPR`) runs an optional bounded evaluator-optimizer fix loop (`runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/main.go::runVerifyFixLoop`) AFTER constraint evaluation and AFTER the ADR-023 self-retry `for{}` loop settles — closing gap G2 (execution-grounded verification) by remediating on top of the #728/#800 detection foundation. The verify command (`executor.verify.command`, served as `verify_command` with `verify_timeout_seconds` / `verify_max_iterations` on the prompt response, operator `--verify-cmd`/`--verify-timeout`/`--verify-max-iterations` win when set) runs against the isolated committed SCOPE-ONLY tree — the **same** drift-excluded HEAD the #728/#800 compile+test gate checks, reached via a fresh `git worktree add --detach` at a throwaway-commit SHA (`runVerifyCommittedTree`, reusing `runVerifyGate`'s `Setpgid` + `cmd.Cancel` process-group SIGKILL so `go test` grandchildren can't hold the output pipe open and block `CombinedOutput`) — **NOT** the dirty working tree, so a drift-excluded test failure (a necessary fake dropped as scope drift, #780/#776) surfaces here exactly as it would in CI rather than false-greening. Each iteration stages scope-only (#581 `StageScoped`), makes a THROWAWAY local commit (`commitVerifyWIP`, bot identity, `--no-verify`, `gpgsign=false`) to materialize a committed-HEAD SHA, verifies, then `git reset --soft HEAD~1` (`gitResetSoftHEAD1`) to undo it — `--soft` moves HEAD without touching the index or working tree, so the converged edits + staged scope survive for either the real push or the next iteration. On PASS it breaks and the push proceeds (the real `CommitAndPush` in `openPRAndShipArtifact` makes the single push commit and runs its own #728/#800 gate); on FAIL with budget remaining it re-invokes the agent in-place with a fix prompt embedding the captured verify output (`verify_fix_reinvoke` log line) and the next iteration re-commits scope-only onto the SAME base — one converged commit, never a stack of fix commits on a bad base. The fix re-invocation is itself bounded against a transient agent-API/transport failure (#804): an `invoker.Invoke` non-nil error is RETRIED IN PLACE up to `maxFixInvokeInfraRetries` (=2) total attempts within the same outer iteration WITHOUT advancing `iter`, so a transient blip (e.g. a #798 Claude usage-limit) does not burn a fix-loop budget unit by re-verifying an unchanged tree — worst-case total fix-Invokes stays hard-bounded at `O(max_iterations × maxFixInvokeInfraRetries)`. Each failed attempt emits a `verify_fix_reinvoke_error` log line + trace event (never silently swallowed), and only a SUCCESSFUL invoke (`fixErr == nil`) folds its events/tokens/model into `res`; exhausting all attempts is an infra failure routed to the non-blocking skip (below), never category-A. **Testcontainers infra-flake absorb (#972)**: a FAILED verify whose output matches the testcontainers container-start-timeout signature (`isTestcontainersStartFlake` — requires `context deadline exceeded` AND a container-start marker: docker.sock path incl. its URL-escaped form, `failed to start container`, `mapped port`, `wait until ready`) is re-run ONCE in place — re-stage, re-commit, re-verify via the existing loop body — WITHOUT invoking the fix agent and WITHOUT advancing `iter`, bounded by a once-per-stage flag; each absorb emits a `verify_infra_flake_retry` log line + trace event. A second flake or a real failure proceeds into the fix-loop / category-A path unchanged; the retry sits after the `reset --soft`, so the #816 fatal-reset and #960 verified-tree invariants are untouched. on FAIL with the budget exhausted (`iter == max_iterations`) it demotes the stage to **category-A** and SKIPS the push. **Terminal, non-compounding (DECISION c2, 2026-06-04):** the loop lives OUTSIDE the self-retry `for{}` loop by construction, so a verify-fix exhaustion can never reach the `continue` that calls `client.RetryStage` — total verify-fix agent invocations are capped at `max_iterations + 1` (initial invoke + up to `max_iterations` fixes) with no multiplicative compounding against the ADR-023 self-retry budget. Each fix re-invocation's `Result.Events` are appended to `res.Events` and its token usage folded into `res` (every *other* `invoker.Invoke` site replaces `res` wholesale; here append is the correct handling), and the OTel stage span (`EmitStage`) is emitted AFTER the loop settles, so a multi-iteration run produces a complete audit trace and an honest cost (ADR-030). A `verify_run` trace event is emitted per attempt (carrying the committed `head_sha`, alongside `command`/`exit_code`/`output`/`outcome`) and a single `verify_summary` event (`{outcome: passed|failed|skipped, iterations, max_iterations}`, emitted EXACTLY ONCE on every exit path — `skipped` carries a `detail` and is the outcome on the infra-abort/non-blocking-skip path, which previously mislabelled itself `failed`, #804 Gap 2) once the loop settles — both are runner *trace* events (like the existing working-tree `verify_run`), NOT Notifier issue-comment surfaces or audit kinds. **Single-shot committed-tree gate (#802).** Default `max_iterations == 0` on the implement push path (`stageType == "implement" && !cfg.noPR`) no longer runs against the dirty working tree — it now runs the configured verify command ONCE against the committed SCOPE-ONLY worktree via `runVerifyGateCommitted` (the same #651 scaffolding: `StageScoped` + throwaway `commitVerifyWIP` + `runVerifyCommittedTree` + `gitResetSoftHEAD1`), demoting to **category-B** by wrapping `gitops.ErrCommittedTestsFailed` on a non-zero exit — except a failure matching the testcontainers start-timeout signature, which gets the same one-shot #972 absorb (re-run `runVerifyCommittedTree` once against the same throwaway headSHA before the reset, both `verify_run` events + the `verify_infra_flake_retry` event in the trace; only a second failure classifies). This is the language-agnostic single-shot twin of the Go-specific #728/#800 committed-test gate, closing the residual #651 did not cover: at `max_iterations == 0` the old path false-greened the drift-excluded class (#780/#776) for any non-Go verify command. The classifier is exit-code-only (the sole universal signal for an arbitrary command); the configurable `executor.verify.skip_on` escape hatch is deferred. Pre-commit infra errors (`StageScoped`/`commitVerifyWIP`/rev-parse, HEAD never moved) are non-blocking skips, but a POST-commit `gitResetSoftHEAD1` failure is FATAL (a swallowed reset would leave HEAD on the throwaway commit and push it into the PR). Only `--no-pr` (dirty working tree, no committed tree/PR to gate) and non-implement stages keep the single-shot **working-tree** demote-on-failure gate (#441 `runVerifyGate`, **category-A**), which is gated off on every implement-push committed-tree path so it can't double-fire inside the self-retry loop. The three verify guards thus partition the `verifyCmd != ""` space with no overlap: working-tree gate = `plan || --no-pr`; committed single-shot gate = `implement && !noPR && max_iterations == 0`; committed fix loop = `implement && !noPR && max_iterations > 0`. An in-loop git/stage infra failure — or a fix re-invocation that fails on infra across all `maxFixInvokeInfraRetries` attempts (#804) — is a non-blocking skip (`verify_fix_skipped`, `verify_summary` outcome `skipped`) — never invent a failure from gate plumbing; the stage proceeds to the real push and its own #728/#800 gate. **Wall-clock bound (ADR-030):** worst-case wall clock is `(max_iterations + 1) × executor.timeout + (max_iterations + 1) × verify.timeout`; `max_iterations` is the control surface and ties to the periodic cost budget. Distinct from `tests_added_or_updated` (touch-check) and `fishhawk_verify_run` (audit-chain integrity).
   - **Verified-SHA invariant (#960).** The SHA that leaves the runner is gate-verified: the committed-tree gates (#651 fix loop / #802 single-shot) verify a THROWAWAY scope-only commit that is reset away, while the real push commit is built independently by `CommitAndPush` (re-staged scope, possibly a freshly-fetched moved base via `FreshFetchBase`) — without enforcement, `stage_state=succeeded` could vouch for a pushed head no gate ever saw (run 07bce059). Both gates now capture the verified commit's **tree object hash** (`git rev-parse <sha>^{tree}` — content-addressed: content + modes + paths, independent of commit metadata) before the reset and return it; the pre-push `VerifyCommit` hook in `openPRAndShipArtifact` compares the real commit's tree against it, ordered LAST after the more specific created-out-of-scope and compile/test gates. Equal trees pass for free (`verified_tree_match` log event); a mismatch (legitimate cause: `origin/<base>` moved between gate and push) logs `verified_tree_mismatch` and triggers exactly ONE strict re-verify of the verify command against the real committed HEAD in the isolated worktree — only an explicit `passed` outcome lets the push proceed (`pushed_tree_reverified`; an infra-skip is NOT a pass, complementing #959), anything else returns `gitops.ErrPushedTreeNotVerified` BEFORE the push (origin untouched, **category-B**, symmetric with `ErrCommitWouldNotCompile`/`ErrCommittedTestsFailed`). Fail-closed corollary: a PASSING gate whose verified tree cannot be resolved is an inconsistent state and returns the same sentinel rather than silently disabling enforcement; the empty-tree/no-enforcement convention is reserved for the no-gate-ran paths (plan stage, `--no-pr`, `verifyCmd` unset, gate skipped). Audit chain: `verify_run` trace events carry `tree_sha` alongside `head_sha`, `CommitAndPushResult.TreeSHA` carries the pushed commit's tree, and the `pull_request_opened` / `implement_fixup_pushed` / `implement_child_pushed` log events are stamped with `verified_tree_sha` + `tree_sha`, so the trail proves `verified_tree_sha == tree_sha` unconditionally (#969): on the reverify-pass path the hook rebinds the effective verified tree to the RE-VERIFIED pushed tree before the stamp sites read it, `pushed_tree_reverified` carries BOTH trees for forensics (`verified_tree_sha` = original gate tree, `tree_sha` = re-verified pushed tree — with `verified_tree_mismatch`, the only places the original gate tree's provenance survives), and the decisive re-verify emits its own `verify_run` structured log event (command/head_sha/tree_sha/exit_code/outcome, output omitted; emitted on failed re-verifies too — the trace bundle is already sealed and uploaded at push time per #742 forward-gating, so logSink is this record's channel).
   - **Index-authority staging + commit-side scope assertion (#980).** `StageScoped` (`runner/internal/gitops/commit.go`) opens with a mixed whole-tree `git reset -q` (index → HEAD, working tree untouched) before enumerating dirty paths, so its postcondition is **index == HEAD + exactly the declared dirty set** by construction. Without it, a file the agent PRE-STAGED with its own `git add` was never unstaged — `git commit` commits the index — so an out-of-scope file landed in the commit while `ScopeDrift` reported it as excluded (run 4c2c6374's 21MB accidental `fishhawk-runner` binary: the exclusion report disagreed with the commit's content). The same pre-staging blinded the #818 created-out-of-scope gate: `UntrackedPaths` uses `git ls-files --others`, which excludes index-resident files, so a staged net-new file was invisible and the run died later on the #960 tree-mismatch path instead of `ErrCreatedOutOfScope` naming the file — the reset makes the file untracked again and restores the gate's reach. Defense-in-depth backstop: on the ScopeFiles path (not the `add -A` fallback), `CommitAndPush` runs a **post-commit out-of-scope assertion** (`assertCommitInScope`) immediately after the commit is created — `git diff-tree -r -z --no-commit-id --name-only <head>` against the (always-parented) new commit, every changed path checked against the SAME declared-set matcher (`scopeMatcher`: exact paths + #824 trailing-slash dir prefixes) StageScoped staged with, one source of truth — and a violation returns `gitops.ErrCommitOutOfScope` naming each violating path BEFORE the push and before the VerifyCommit gates (origin untouched, **category-B**, symmetric with `ErrCommitWouldNotCompile`/`ErrPushedTreeNotVerified`). This is the commit-side complement of #960's tree equivalence: #960 proves the pushed tree is the gate-verified tree, #980 proves the committed content is the declared scope and nothing else, so the drift report can never again disagree with the commit. Advisory layer: at the `scope_drift` emit site the runner classifies each drift path with `isBinaryArtifactDrift` (executable with size > 5MiB, or the Go module-binary shape `cmd/<name>/<name>`) and emits a log-only `scope_drift_binary_artifact` event naming path + size — false negatives acceptable; the assertion and the #818 gate are the enforcement layers.
   - **Gate-phase credential posture (ADR-029 #650 item 4).** Every gate above executes *committed agent-authored code* — `go vet`/`go test` (and the `go work edit` enumeration) inside `verifyCommittedTreeCompiles`, and the spec `executor.verify.command` (`sh -c …`) in `runVerifyGate`/`runVerifyCommittedTree`. Those subprocesses run under a **stripped, default-deny allow-listed env** (`runner/cmd/fishhawk-runner/gateenv.go::sanitizedGateEnv`): only PATH/HOME/system essentials and the Go toolchain vars (`GO*`/`CGO_*`/CC/CXX) survive, so the runner's GitHub App installation token (`FISHHAWK_GITHUB_TOKEN`/`GITHUB_TOKEN`/`GH_TOKEN`), the agent API keys (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`/`OPENAI_API_KEY`), and the MCP backend token (`FISHHAWK_API_TOKEN`) are NOT visible to agent code — defusing the lethal-trifecta shape (agent code + network + runner creds). The allow-list is default-deny so a secret env var added to the runner later is dropped automatically; an explicit denylist is belt-and-suspenders on top. The git-plumbing exec sites (worktree add/remove, rev-parse, reset, add) keep the inherited env — they run on runner-controlled refs and need git credential context. A gate exec site added later must opt into `sanitizedGateEnv` to preserve this invariant. (`GOPROXY` and other GO* values are preserved for dependency resolution, but embedded URL userinfo — `scheme://user:pass@host` credentials an operator may put in a GOPROXY/GOSUMDB URL — is redacted per comma/`|`-separated entry by `gateenv.go::redactGoEnvUserinfo` before it reaches gate code, keeping the proxy host/path usable while closing the #940 leak; non-URL GO* forms pass through byte-identical.)
   - **Empty captured diff → retryable category C (#691/#692).** A *genuine* constraint hit (forbidden/allowed paths, max_files, ci-not-green) stays category B. But an implement stage whose bundle carries a *present-but-empty* `git_diff` event (zero changed files, the #691 staging-bug signature) fails only the `tests_added_or_updated` required-outcome — a capture/staging miss, not a policy breach. The trace handler (`backend/internal/server/trace.go::noDiffCaptured`) reclassifies that exact case to a retryable category C with a `no_diff_captured:` reason, so the existing `failed → pending` retry path gives operators an escape hatch (an un-redrivable B would also doom a fan-out parent). The carve-out is strict: any other violation — notably an `invalid pattern` config error a malformed glob emits even on an empty diff — keeps the failure as category B. A bundle with *no* `git_diff` event at all remains the `no_diff_in_bundle` skip→pass path, untouched.
6. **Review stage** — Backend awaits human approval on the PR plus blocking checks (`ci_pass`, `fishhawk_audit_complete`).
7. **Merge** — On merge, backend writes the final audit entry and closes the run.

Failure categories (per §6) are captured in the `audit_entries` table with `category` ∈ {A: agent, B: constraint, C: infra, D: approval timeout}. Re-execution is allowed for all four; idempotency keys prevent double-fire.

### 4.1 Self-retry decision tree (ADR-023)

When `executor.agent_self_retry: true` is set on a stage in the workflow spec, the runner applies a four-gate decision after each invocation before exiting with failure:

1. **Spec opt-in** — `cfg.agentSelfRetry == true` (surfaced via the prompt-fetch response). Stages without the flag always exit on failure.
2. **Signing key available** — `issuedKey != nil`. Only the `--fetch-prompt` path issues the key before the invoke loop; without it the gate closes and no retry fires. Guarantees the runner can authenticate the POST.
3. **Remaining budget > 0** — `maxRetriesSnapshot - retryAttempt - selfRetryCount > 0`. `maxRetriesSnapshot` and `retryAttempt` are snapshotted from the run row at prompt-fetch time and returned in the prompt response. `selfRetryCount` tracks in-process retries on the current runner invocation.
4. **Failure category A or C** — category B (constraint/policy) and D (approval timeout) are not retried in-process; those require a spec change or human action.

**Call path on retry**: runner calls `POST /v0/stages/{id}/retry` (signed with the Ed25519 per-run key, same mechanism as trace upload). The backend transitions the stage `failed → pending` and the orchestrator fires `workflow_dispatch`, launching a second GitHub Actions runner. Meanwhile the current runner process emits a `stage_self_retry` log line, resets its result state, and re-invokes the agent in the same for-loop iteration.

**Known v0 limitation**: the `workflow_dispatch` fires a second GHA runner that also fetches the stage prompt (the dispatched state is not blocked by the prompt handler's state guard) and begins a concurrent agent invocation. The two agents race; whichever uploads its trace bundle last wins at the backend's idempotent-store layer. No v0 guard prevents this; the orphaned runner's trace upload races with the winner's. An integration test asserting only one trace-upload call per retried stage is deliberately deferred.

### 4.2 Plan-review-agent invocation (ADR-027)

When a stage's `reviewers.agent > 0` is configured in the workflow spec, the backend invokes that many review agents inline inside `handleShipPlan` (in `backend/internal/server/plan.go`), after the plan artifact is stored and validated but before the stage transitions to `awaiting_approval`. Each review agent receives the plan artifact and the originating issue body via the `plan_review` prompt (`buildPlanReview` in `backend/internal/prompt/prompt.go`) and is instructed to return only a structured verdict JSON — re-planning and proposing edits are explicitly forbidden.

**Invocation flow:**

1. Plan artifact POSTed to `POST /v0/runs/{id}/plan?stage_id=…`.
2. Backend verifies signature, validates against `standard_v1`, stores in `artifacts`.
3. If `stage.reviewers.agent > 0`: spawn N review-agent invocations synchronously, each receiving the plan artifact + issue body via `prompt.Build("plan_review", …)`.
4. Self-review guard: if the review agent's model matches `plan.GeneratedBy.Model`, log WARN (ADR-027 §5 — no hard block in v0).
5. For each review response, decode the `ReviewVerdict` JSON, append a `plan_reviewed` audit entry (category string `"plan_reviewed"`, payload `PlanReviewedPayload` from `backend/internal/planreview/review.go`).
6. Authority check: if `ResolveAuthority(reviewers) == AuthorityGating` (i.e. `agent>0 && human==0`) and any verdict is `reject`, the stage stays in its current state rather than advancing to `awaiting_approval`.
7. Otherwise `handleShipPlan` drives the plan stage's terminal advance (`advancePlanStageTerminal`): `running → awaiting_approval`, or `running → succeeded` + an orchestrator `Advance` for a gateless plan stage.

**Plan-stage advancement invariant** (#603): a plan stage's terminal transition is driven by a valid `standard_v1` plan artifact landing via the plan-upload handler (`handleShipPlan`), **not** by trace upload alone. The runner ships both trace variants before the plan, so `advanceStageAfterTrace` (`backend/internal/server/trace.go`) leaves a plan stage in `running` until `planArtifactExists` confirms a plan is stored — guarding against a gated plan stage reaching `awaiting_approval` with nothing to review when the later plan-ship fails `standard_v1` validation. On an invalid plan, `handleShipPlan` fails the stage category-B (`running → failed`) and calls `advanceAfterFailure` so the run walks to terminal `failed` rather than stranding. The transition is idempotent (same-state re-application is a repo-layer no-op), so a future plan-first upload ordering — where the trace handler finds the artifact and advances first — does not double-fault. When `ArtifactRepo` is unwired (minimal config), `planArtifactExists` returns true so the gate is a no-op and the prior trace-driven advance is preserved.

**Push-and-open-pr implement advancement invariant** (#742): mirroring the plan-stage gate, an implement stage that will open a PR has its terminal transition driven by the **`/pull-request` upload** (`handleShipPullRequest`), **not** by trace upload alone. The runner ships the trace *before* it commits/pushes/opens the PR, so advancing to `awaiting_approval` on trace upload would strand the run at the review gate with a null `pull_request_url` if the PR-open step then fails (the b6811dc9 zombie). The runner stamps `push_and_open_pr` in the bundle manifest (`bundle.ManifestData` / `bundle.Manifest`, both `omitempty`, lockstep across the two modules — see the trace-bundle manifest row in §10) for every standalone implement stage (not `--no-pr`, not a decomposed child). `advanceStageAfterTrace` (`trace.go::pushAndOpenPRGated`) then leaves the stage in `running` when the flag is set **and** the bundle carries a non-empty `git_diff` (a commit + PR-open will follow) — emitting the `runtime_observed` calibration row before the early return, since the bundle timing is only available at trace time. The `/pull-request` upload becomes the authoritative terminal driver: the **success** body advances `running → awaiting_approval`/`succeeded` (+ orchestrator `Advance` for a gateless stage) after stamping `run.pull_request_url` (`advanceImplementStageAfterPR`); the **failure** body (`{outcome:"failed", category, reason}`, `failPullRequestStage`) fails the stage (category `C` retryable / `B` re-scope) + advances + appends a `pull_request_failed` audit entry, so the run never reaches review with a null PR. The runner reports that failure from the implement post-processing block (`main.go::reportPullRequestFailure`, gated on the same standalone-implement condition) when `openPRAndShipArtifact` errors — unifying #728's `ErrCommitWouldNotCompile` (category B) into this report path. An **empty** diff is the no-changes carve-out: the agent made no edits, opens no PR, and never POSTs `/pull-request`, so `pushAndOpenPRGated` returns false and the stage advances via the trace handler as before (gating it would hang the stage in `running`). Non-implement stages and a false flag (older bundles, `--no-pr`) are byte-identical to the prior trace-driven advance. **Decomposed-child push gate (#771)**: a decomposed child opens no PR (the parent run opens one consolidated PR, ADR-032) but still commits + pushes onto the shared branch *after* the trace ships, so the same zombie hole applied — a child push failure left the child stage at terminal `succeeded` with no code on the shared branch, which the childcompletion sweeper would then consolidate into a PR silently missing that child's work. The runner stamps a separate `push_to_shared_branch` manifest flag (mutually exclusive with `push_and_open_pr`) for decomposed children; `trace.go::childPushGated` mirrors `pushAndOpenPRGated` to leave the child stage in `running`, and the `/pull-request` upload drives the terminal transition via a new `{outcome:"pushed", branch, head_sha, base_sha}` success variant (`succeedChildPushStage`: running → terminal + `child_pushed` audit entry, no PR artifact) or the existing `{outcome:"failed"}` variant on a push failure (category `C` retryable via the #698 parent-park). **Fix-up push gate (#794)**: a fix-up re-dispatch (#762) opens no new PR (it commits onto the EXISTING PR branch *after* the trace ships) but the same zombie hole applied in its worst form — a fix-up stamped *neither* `push_and_open_pr` *nor* `push_to_shared_branch`, so it terminalized at trace upload *before* its commit/push, and a push or `#728` compile-gate failure was silently swallowed: the stage stayed `succeeded`, the implement re-review approved an *unlanded* diff, and the #788 fix-up recovery never fired (the stage never went terminal-failed). The runner now stamps a third mutually-exclusive `push_fixup` manifest flag for fix-up passes; `trace.go::fixupPushGated` mirrors `childPushGated` to leave the fix-up stage in `running` (the advisory implement re-review still fires at trace time on the bundle diff — only the TERMINAL transition is deferred), and the `/pull-request` upload drives the terminal transition via a new `{outcome:"fixup_pushed", branch, head_sha, base_sha}` success variant (`succeedFixupPushStage`: running → terminal + `fixup_pushed` audit entry, no PR artifact, idempotency-guarded on `(stage_id, head_sha)`) or the existing `{outcome:"failed"}` variant on a push/compile-gate failure — which now genuinely reaches `failPullRequestStage → maybeRecoverFixupFailure`, restoring the run to its pre-fix-up review gate with the original mergeable PR intact. **Dangling-PR close on a gating implement-review reject (#877)**: a gating agent implement-review (`reviewers.agent>0`, `human==0`) runs SYNCHRONOUSLY during the raw-trace upload (`advanceStageAfterTrace::runImplementReviews`) and fails the implement stage category-B with reason `implement_review_rejected: …` (hoisted to the const `implementReviewGatingRejectReason`) BEFORE the runner — which has no view of that verdict — opens its PR and POSTs `/pull-request`. By then the stage is terminally failed, so `handleShipPullRequest` records the PR artifact + `pull_request_opened` audit + URL backfill (the honest open) but `advanceImplementStageAfterPR` no-ops (stage not running), leaving the rejected change with an open PR that will never merge. After the backfill and BEFORE the lineage guard, the handler detects that exact state — implement + `failed` + category-B + the `implementReviewGatingRejectPrefix` reason prefix (same source of truth as the trace failure site; other category-B sources are excluded) — and calls `closePRAfterGatingReject`: it posts a short explanatory PR comment, closes the PR via the new `githubclient.ClosePullRequest` (`PATCH .../pulls/{number}` body `{"state":"closed"}`, mirroring `ForceUpdateRef`), and writes a `pull_request_closed_after_review_reject` audit entry (system actor, payload `{run_id, stage_id, artifact_id, pr_number, pr_url, failure_reason}`). Fail-open throughout: GitHub unconfigured, a nil `installation_id`, an unparseable repo, or a close error WARNs and skips — the stage is already failed, so a failed close must never 500 the handler. Advisory reviews dispatch detached and never reject-fail the stage, so they never reach this state and are byte-identical (PR-open timing for the advisory flow is unchanged; ADR-036/#876 enforces at the landing gate, not at PR-open).

**Authority resolution** (`backend/internal/planreview/review.go::ResolveAuthority`):

| `reviewers.agent` | `reviewers.human` | Mode |
|---|---|---|
| > 0 | == 0 | `gating` — agent rejection blocks stage advancement |
| > 0 | > 0 | `advisory` — agent verdict recorded but human gate is authoritative |
| == 0 | any | `gateless` — no agent review; today's default |

**Verdict types** (closed set per ADR-027): `approve`, `approve_with_concerns`, `reject`.

**Audit category `plan_reviewed`**: one entry per review-agent invocation, payload shape `PlanReviewedPayload{ReviewerKind, ReviewerModel, Authority, Verdict, Concerns[], FreeForm}`. Surfaced by `fishhawk_get_plan` (MCP tool) in `GetPlanOutput.Reviews[]`.

**Audit category `plan_review_started`** (#600): a single best-effort entry emitted by `runPlanReviews` at dispatch — **only** on the branch where a reviewer will actually run (`reviewers.agent > 0` AND a `PlanReviewer` is wired), never for the gateless (`agent == 0`) or nil-reviewer (`plan_review_skipped`) branches. Payload shape `ReviewStartedPayload{ConfiguredAgents, Authority}` (`backend/internal/planreview/review.go`). It is emitted synchronously before the per-reviewer loop, so its audit sequence always precedes every `plan_reviewed` entry under both gating (synchronous) and advisory (detached) authority. This is the MCP-readable proxy that lets a consumer distinguish *configured + running* (a `started` entry with no terminal entry) from *none configured* (no entry at all) without re-fetching the workflow spec — the basis for the `review_status` = `pending` state surfaced by `fishhawk_get_run_status` / `fishhawk_get_plan` and waited on by `fishhawk_await_review` (see the MCP row in §10 "Where to look"). **Completeness is count-gated (#1127)**: the payload's `ConfiguredAgents` is the threshold the MCP `review_status` resolves `complete` against — `review_status` stays `pending` until `landed_terminal >= configured_agents` (ANY terminal kind — `plan_reviewed` / `plan_review_failed` / `plan_review_skipped` — counts toward the round), the SAME completeness rule `checkPlanReviewSettled` uses for the approval gate, so `await_review` / `get_run_status` no longer report a partial heterogeneous result (a poll that catches the window after the first of N reviewers landed but before the rest finish now reports `pending`, not `complete` with one verdict). An absent or non-positive `ConfiguredAgents` (old/malformed payload) degrades to the prior complete-on-first-verdict predicate.

**Production wiring** (`backend/internal/anthropic/`, wired in `backend/cmd/fishhawkd/serve.go`):

`serve.go` wires `cfg.PlanReviewers`, a `server.ReviewerSet` (resolved by the pure `resolvePlanReviewers` helper so the wiring seam is unit-testable; every configured adapter logs at startup). Since #955 ALL configured backends are concurrently available — the set replaces the old single precedence-selected `cfg.PlanReviewer`:

- **`ReviewerSet.Default()`** serves the bare `reviewers.agent: N` count form by the historical four-way precedence (unchanged behavior — the default adapter is invoked N times):
  1. **`FISHHAWKD_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set** → `anthropic.NewReviewer` (production SDK adapter, #572). Wins over everything below.
  2. **else `FISHHAWKD_ENABLE_LOCAL_CLAUDE_REVIEWER=true`** → `claudecode.NewReviewer` (local-mode subprocess adapter, #575). See **Local-mode wire-up** below.
  3. **else `FISHHAWKD_ENABLE_CODEX_REVIEWER=true`** → `codex.NewReviewer` (Codex subprocess adapter, #844). See **Codex reviewer wire-up** below.
  4. **else** → `Default()` returns nil; review invocations are skipped (the #574 degradation below).
- **`ReviewerSet.For(provider, model)`** resolves one entry of the heterogeneous `reviewers.agents` list (#955, see `docs/spec/workflow-v0.md` § Plan reviewers) to its adapter, constructed with the spec-requested model (empty → that provider's deployment default model; codex reasoning effort stays deployment-level). Adapters are stateless config-struct wrappers (`NewReviewer` does no I/O), so per-resolve construction is cheap. An unconfigured provider errors: gating stages fail dispatch up front at run create, advisory stages degrade per-reviewer with a `plan_review_failed` / `implement_review_failed` entry carrying the resolve error while the loop continues.

The review loops (`runPlanReviewLoop` / `runImplementReviewLoop`) iterate a resolved `[]reviewerInvocation` (one per declared reviewer) instead of repeating a single adapter; per-invocation audit entries, reviewer-cost recording, and the per-invocation self-review guard are unchanged. The effective agent count everywhere (authority table, skip/started payloads) is `spec.ReviewersConfig.AgentCount()` — `len(agents)` when the list is present, else the bare `agent` integer.

| Env var | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `FISHHAWKD_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | `` (empty) | Anthropic API key; empty disables the SDK adapter |
| `FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_MODEL` | `claude-sonnet-4-6` | Model used for each SDK review invocation |
| `FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_MAX_TOKENS` | `4096` | Maximum output tokens per review call (both adapters) |
| `FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_MAX_RETRIES` | `1` | Bounds the subprocess reviewers' in-adapter transient-crash retry (#620). Counts **retries, not attempts** (N retries => N+1 attempts). Unset defaults to 1 (one retry); an explicit `0` disables retry (single attempt); negatives clamp to 0. Governs the claudecode **and** codex adapters — the anthropic SDK adapter has no retry field. |
| `FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_TIMEOUT` | `300s` | **Floor** of the size-aware review budget (#747; was a fixed per-invocation timeout before). The effective per-invocation deadline is computed at the server call site as `Floor + PerKB*ceil(promptBytes/1024)`, clamped to `[Floor, Cap]`, and applied to the reviewer context; the adapter honours that incoming deadline rather than capping at its own `Config.Timeout`. The 300s floor preserves the #606 value so small plans behave exactly as before, while large diffs (the #742/#745 13-file/~600-line case that was killed mid-inference, dropping the verdict) scale up. Detached from the upload request via `context.WithoutCancel` since #584, so the budget is the only deadline in the chain. Distinct from the ~1s immediate-crash mode owned by #620. See **Review dispatch: advisory-async vs. gating-sync** below. |
| `FISHHAWKD_REVIEW_BUDGET_PER_KB` | `10s` | Per-KB allowance added to the review-budget floor per kilobyte of prompt (#747), rounded up to the next KB. Set to `0` to collapse the budget to a flat floor — restoring the pre-#747 fixed-timeout behaviour without a redeploy (the operational rollback lever). |
| `FISHHAWKD_REVIEW_BUDGET_CAP` | `1200s` | Hard ceiling on the size-aware review budget (#747), bounding the worst-case synchronous gating wait for a very large diff. A non-positive value disables the ceiling. Floor always wins on conflict (a cap below the floor still yields at least the floor). |
| `FISHHAWKD_ENABLE_LOCAL_CLAUDE_REVIEWER` | `false` | Opt-in local-mode subprocess adapter; ignored when the API key is set |
| `FISHHAWKD_LOCAL_CLAUDE_BINARY` | `claude` | Executable name/path for the local-mode `claude` CLI |
| `FISHHAWKD_LOCAL_CLAUDE_MODEL` | `claude-sonnet-4-6` | Model the local-mode `claude` CLI uses for review |
| `FISHHAWKD_ENABLE_CODEX_REVIEWER` | `false` | Opt-in Codex subprocess adapter (#844); lower precedence than the API key and the local-claude flag |
| `FISHHAWKD_CODEX_BINARY` | `codex` | Executable name/path for the Codex reviewer CLI |
| `FISHHAWKD_CODEX_MODEL` | `` (empty) | Model the Codex reviewer runs, passed as `codex exec --model`; also the model identifier recorded for Codex reviews and compared to the plan's `GeneratedBy.Model` by the self-review guard (the two now match). Empty omits the flag and inherits the host `~/.codex` config |
| `FISHHAWKD_CODEX_REASONING_EFFORT` | `` (empty) | Reasoning effort for the Codex reviewer, passed as `-c model_reasoning_effort=<effort>` (e.g. low/medium/high); empty omits the override and inherits the host `~/.codex` config |
| `FISHHAWKD_OPENAI_API_KEY` | `` (empty) | OpenAI key forwarded as `OPENAI_API_KEY` to the Codex subprocess; empty is fine when Codex is authenticated via a ChatGPT login on the host |

**Local-mode wire-up** (`backend/internal/claudecode/`, #575). The local-mode adapter is the dogfood sibling of `anthropic.Reviewer`: instead of provisioning an API key, it spawns the operator's existing `claude` CLI as a subprocess (`claude --print --output-format json --model <model> -p <prompt>`), inheriting `os.Environ()` so the operator's subscription auth or already-present `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is reused with zero new plumbing. It decodes the CLI's single JSON envelope, reads the `result` field, and validates the verdict against the same closed set (`approve` / `approve_with_concerns` / `reject`) as the SDK adapter.

The retry budget (`FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_MAX_RETRIES`, see the env-var table) is wired through a post-construction `(*claudecode.Reviewer).SetMaxRetries` call rather than `claudecode.Config.MaxRetries` directly: `NewClient` normalises a zero `MaxRetries` to 1, so passing the env value through the constructor could never express *disable*. `serve.go` therefore constructs the reviewer, then calls `SetMaxRetries(*planReviewMaxRetries)` to assign the env-resolved value past that normalisation. The result: unset → 1 (one retry, unchanged from #620's code default), an explicit `0` → retry disabled (single attempt), `N` → N+1 attempts, and a negative value clamps to 0.

Recommended local config for dogfooding (copy-pasteable):

```sh
export FISHHAWKD_ENABLE_LOCAL_CLAUDE_REVIEWER=true
export FISHHAWKD_LOCAL_CLAUDE_MODEL=claude-sonnet-4-6
export FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_TIMEOUT=300
```

Large-plan expectation (#606): small plans review in ~60–90s, but large backend `standard_v1` plans (10+ files) need the wider budget — the old 60s default timed them out and the agent verdict never landed. Because advisory plan review is async/detached (#584), a longer budget blocks nothing: it only lengthens the worst-case `pending` window that re-polling `fishhawk_get_run_status` (the poll-authoritative terminal source, #879) surfaces via `poll_interval_seconds`, and that `fishhawk_await_review` (default 360s since #878, the optional non-stranding convenience block per ADR-036 #874) already returns gracefully (#600). The override above is redundant with the new 300s code default but kept in the copy-pasteable block for explicitness. This is the genuine timeout-on-large-plans mode only; the separate ~1s immediate-crash mode is owned by #620.

The review model (`claude-sonnet-4-6`) deliberately differs from the plan **author's** typical default (Opus): the plan-review prompt's self-review guard keys on the model identifier, so reviewing with a different model than authored keeps the self-review WARN meaningful rather than firing on every plan. The adapter returns the configured `FISHHAWKD_LOCAL_CLAUDE_MODEL` verbatim as the verdict's model identifier (the CLI envelope does not reliably echo the model), keeping that guard deterministic.

Accepted local-mode trade-offs vs. the SDK adapter: (a) softer verdict-shape guarantee — the subprocess has tool access via the `claude` CLI and only the prompt forbids repo grepping, so a non-compliant agent degrades to a per-call `reject` rather than silent corruption; (b) self-review WARN noise if the review model matches the author model (mitigated by the recommended config above); (c) no prompt caching — the full prompt is sent as one `-p` argument with no system/user split; (d) ~1–2s process-spawn overhead per review.

**Codex reviewer wire-up** (`backend/internal/codex/`, #844). The Codex adapter is the OpenAI sibling of `claudecode.Reviewer` and the reviewer analog of the runner's Codex **executor** (`runner/internal/agent/codex`, #840/#842): it spawns `codex exec --json --skip-git-repo-check <prompt>` for inference-only review and reads one JSON event per line from stdout. The verdict body is the final `item.completed` line whose nested item is an `agent_message` (its `text`); per-turn token usage is summed from each `turn.completed` line's `usage` block and normalized at the adapter boundary (#1010): the CLI's raw `input_tokens` is cache-INCLUSIVE (it counts `cached_input_tokens`), so the adapter reports `Usage.InputTokens` = summed input − summed cached, clamped at 0 — the cache-EXCLUSIVE fresh count every adapter now reports — with the cached sum in `Usage.CachedInputTokens` and the turn count in `Usage.Turns` (#995); `reasoning_output_tokens` is added to the output side. Two advisory WARN ceilings watch the normalized figures in `recordReviewerCost`: 100k on fresh input (a genuine context-assembly blowup) and 500k on total input (fresh + cached — a runaway total context that heavy caching keeps off the fresh ceiling). The event shapes are pinned against the installed Codex CLI (codex-cli 0.137.0, the same version the executor pinned) and encoded in the fake-binary test fixtures so a future CLI drift fails a test rather than silently dropping the reviewer. **Scratch-dir cwd contract (#995):** each invocation runs from a fresh EMPTY `os.MkdirTemp` directory set as `cmd.Dir` (removed after the call). The review prompt forbids tool use but cannot prevent it — `codex exec` non-interactive runs read-only shell commands without approval, so an unset `cmd.Dir` inherits fishhawkd's cwd (the repo checkout, in the dogfood loop) and an exploring reviewer reads it across many turns, each turn re-sending the full growing conversation (the observed ~400k-input-token review, ~100x the claude reviewer). The empty cwd makes exploration fruitless; a `MkdirTemp` failure FAILS CLOSED as a per-invocation error (degrading the advisory review gracefully via the per-invocation failure path) rather than silently regaining the unbounded workspace. `--skip-git-repo-check` is required because the scratch dir is not a git repo (and the backend has no checkout anyway); unlike the executor the reviewer needs **no** `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox` (confirmed against a real `codex exec --json` from a non-repo dir, which returns cleanly without blocking on approvals). The decoded `text` is run through the shared `planreview.DecodeVerdict` (#739 fence-strip + #889 invalid-escape tolerance), validated against the same closed verdict set, and the summed usage is attached with `Known=false` when no `turn.completed` usage line appeared (no silent $0, #681/#682). `OPENAI_API_KEY` is forwarded explicitly from `FISHHAWKD_OPENAI_API_KEY` on top of the inherited `os.Environ()`; an empty key is not an error (ChatGPT-login auth on the host is supported). The retry budget is wired through `SetMaxRetries` exactly as the claudecode adapter does. This enables the **Claude main, Codex reviewer** topology.

**Prompt-caching strategy**: the `plan_review` prompt (`buildPlanReview` in `backend/internal/prompt/prompt.go`) is split at `prompt.PlanReviewSplitMarker` (`"\n### Plan artifact\n\n"`). Everything before the marker — the role-constraint preamble — is placed in the Anthropic system block with `cache_control: {type: ephemeral}`. Everything from the marker onward (plan artifact + issue context + verdict schema) is placed in the user message. This keeps the cached prefix stable across calls for the same fishhawkd process lifetime while allowing the variable plan content to change per invocation.

**Nil-reviewer degradation (#574).** When a workflow declares `reviewers.agent > 0` but `server.Config.PlanReviewer` is nil (no `FISHHAWKD_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set — see Production wiring above), the behavior is two-tier, keyed on authority:

- **Gating mode** (`agent>0, human==0`): rejected before any run row is minted, on both entry points. `handleCreateRun` (`backend/internal/server/runs.go`) iterates the resolved spec's plan stages and, on an agent-gated stage with a nil reviewer, writes a `run_rejected_misconfigured` **global-chain** audit entry (`AppendGlobalChained` — no run row exists yet) and returns `400 plan_reviewer_unconfigured`. The webhook-dispatcher path (`Dispatcher.Handle`, gated on a plain `PlanReviewerConfigured` bool wired from `cfg.PlanReviewer != nil` to avoid the server→webhook import cycle) applies the symmetric guard as of #577: it writes the same `run_rejected_misconfigured` global-chain entry and refuses to dispatch. Because the dispatcher returns 202 to GitHub and cannot surface a 400, the audit entry plus a WARN log are the rejection surface (a customer-facing issue comment is a deferred follow-up). Either way a run that could never satisfy its own gate is never minted.
- **Advisory mode** (`agent>0, human>0`): allowed through — the human gate remains authoritative. At plan upload, `runPlanReviews` (`backend/internal/server/plan.go`) emits a `plan_review_skipped` audit entry and the stage continues to `awaiting_approval`.

**Audit category `plan_review_skipped`** (#574): emitted by `runPlanReviews` when the spec requested agent review (`reviewers.agent>0`) but no `PlanReviewer` is wired. Payload shape `ReviewSkippedPayload{Reason, ConfiguredAgents, Authority}` (`backend/internal/planreview/review.go`); `Reason` is `reviewer_not_configured`. Emitted for both gating and advisory authority so that any run which reaches plan upload with a nil reviewer is auditable rather than silent; in advisory mode this is the primary trail, and in gating mode it is a defensive backstop now that both create paths reject gating+nil before a run is minted. Surfaced by `fishhawk_get_plan` as a synthesized review with `verdict: "skipped"` and the recorded reason.

**Audit category `run_rejected_misconfigured`** (#574, #577): global-chain entry written when an agent-gated plan stage is rejected for a missing reviewer — by `handleCreateRun` on the run-create path and by `Dispatcher.Handle` on the webhook-dispatcher path. Payload names the offending stage, workflow_id, repo, and configured agent count; the dispatcher additionally carries `delivery_id` and `event` for GitHub-trigger correlation. On the dispatcher path, when the trigger is an issue (labeled or `/fishhawk run`), the refusal is also surfaced to the customer as an issue comment via `IssueNotifier.NotifyRunRejected` (#599) — the audit entry above stays the canonical operator-side record, the comment is best-effort customer-facing UX naming both fixes (configure the server reviewer, or change the stage `reviewers`).

A startup WARN fires at `fishhawkd` boot (`backend/cmd/fishhawkd/serve.go`) whenever the resolved `PlanReviewer` is nil (neither the API key nor the local-mode flag is set), describing both degradation modes; it disappears once either adapter is wired.

**Review dispatch: advisory-async vs. gating-sync (#584).** Both `runPlanReviews` and `runImplementReviews` resolve the stage's review config on the request goroutine (cheap reads: `GetRun`, `resolveStageReviewers`, plan parse/load, prompt build), then split on authority:

- **Advisory** (`agent>0, human>0`): the per-reviewer loop is dispatched on a **detached** context (`context.WithoutCancel(ctx)`) in a goroutine tracked by `Server.bgReviews`, and the handler returns its response (`201`/`202`) immediately without waiting on the reviewer. This is the fix for the reviewer being SIGKILLed at the runner's upload-client timeout: the review previously ran on `r.Context()`, which the server cancels when the runner's client closes the connection (default was 60s), so `context.WithTimeout(r.Context(), FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_TIMEOUT)` fired at `min(parent, budget) = 60s` and `exec.CommandContext` killed `claude`. Detaching lets the review run to its own size-aware review budget (#747; floor `FISHHAWKD_PLAN_REVIEW_TIMEOUT`, scaled by prompt size). Because the detached `reviewCtx` carries no deadline, that per-invocation budget — applied at the call site via `context.WithTimeout(reviewCtx, ReviewBudget.Budget(len(promptText)))` and honoured by the adapter — is the only deadline in the chain (`context.WithTimeout` takes the earlier of parent/child, so a larger budget can't extend a shorter parent that doesn't exist here). The advisory verdict is non-blocking (the human gate stays authoritative), so it landing after the stage reaches `awaiting_approval` is correct.
- **Gating** (`agent>0, human==0`): the review loop runs **synchronously** on the (still detached) context. Synchronicity is required because a reject must transition the stage to `failed` (category B) *before* the trace handler's terminal `awaiting_approval` advance — the block relies on the `awaiting_approval → failed` (plan path) / `running → failed` (implement path) state-machine edges. The detached context still removes the client-disconnect clamp; it just doesn't return early.

**Graceful-shutdown drain.** `Server.Shutdown` waits on `bgReviews` after `http.Shutdown` returns, bounded by the same shutdown context, so in-flight advisory reviews finish (or are abandoned at the deadline) rather than being dropped. `Server.waitBackgroundReviews()` is the test sync point for deterministic async assertions. The runner's upload-client default timeout was also raised 60s→120s as defense-in-depth (`runner/internal/upload/upload.go`), but the decoupling — not the bump — is the real fix; a retried upload short-circuits at `GetByHash`.

**Plan-approval completion gate (ADR-036 / #875).** Because an advisory plan review is dispatched **detached** (above), the plan stage reaches `awaiting_approval` while the reviewer is still running — so a human could approve before reading the agent's verdict. `checkPlanReviewSettled` (`backend/internal/server/approvals.go`) closes that window: on a plan-stage **approve**, when the stage declares `reviewers.agent > 0` AND a `plan_review_started` entry is present AND fewer than the configured agent count of TERMINAL verdicts have landed, it refuses with `409 agent_review_pending` (`details`: `stage_id`, `configured_agents`, `landed_terminal`). ANY terminal kind counts toward the unblock — `plan_reviewed`, `plan_review_failed` (the #747 budget-kill emits this), or `plan_review_skipped` — so a timed-out or skipped reviewer never strands the gate. The gate is placed **before** `ApprovalRepo.Submit`, so a refused approval records no row and the operator's poll-then-retry (idempotent) flows normally once verdicts land. A hard backstop — `planReviewBackstop(agentCount) = ReviewBudget.Cap × max(1, agentCount)`, measured from the earliest `plan_review_started` — ALLOWS the approval once elapsed even with no terminal verdict, writing a `plan_review_backstop_elapsed` audit entry (actor `system`; payload `{stage_id, configured_agents, landed_terminal, started_at, elapsed_seconds}`), so a reviewer that dies emitting nothing can never brick approvals. Every read fails OPEN (WARN-log, allow) mirroring `checkPlanBudget`. `reviewers.agent == 0` (no agent reviewer) and a configured-but-not-dispatched stage (no `plan_review_started`) are byte-for-byte the pre-ADR-036 path. Surfaced through `fishhawk_approve_plan` as a typed poll-until-landed error carrying the landed/configured counts (no auto-retry in the tool).

**Implement-review / merge completion gate (ADR-036 / #876).** The same completion-gate discipline extends to the implement-review (ADR-027) / merge gate, but at the **merge-driven resolution** path rather than an HTTP approve. `checkImplementReviewSettled` (`backend/internal/server/pullrequest_review_events.go`) gates `resolveReviewStageOnMerge`: on a merge (`merged==true`) of a run that has a review stage, when the IMPLEMENT stage declares `reviewers.agent > 0` AND an `implement_review_started` entry is present AND fewer than the configured count of TERMINAL `implement_reviewed`/`implement_review_failed`/`implement_review_skipped` verdicts have landed, it refuses to resolve the stage to `succeeded` and leaves the run **parked in `awaiting_approval`**. The merge reconciler (`backend/internal/mergereconciler/`) re-scans parked review stages each tick and routes them back through `ResolveReviewFromPollState → resolveReviewStageOnMerge`, so the gate retries identically on both the `pull_request.closed` webhook (one-shot) and the poll (retrying), and **composes with the #862 lineage re-check** already on the tick path (both must pass for the stage to resolve). ANY terminal kind unblocks (a budget-killed reviewer emits `implement_review_failed` — see `trace.go`), so a timed-out reviewer never strands the merge. A hard backstop reusing `planReviewBackstop(agentCount) = ReviewBudget.Cap × max(1, agentCount)` (measured from the earliest `implement_review_started`) ALLOWS the resolution once elapsed even with no terminal verdict, recording an `implement_review_backstop_elapsed` audit entry (actor `system`; payload `{stage_id, configured_agents, landed_terminal, started_at, elapsed_seconds}`) exactly once (the resolve completes the run, so the reconciler stops re-polling). Every read fails OPEN (WARN-log, resolve); `reviewers.agent == 0` and a not-dispatched stage are byte-for-byte the pre-ADR-036 merge resolution. **Behavioral change — deferred `pr_merged` audit:** the gate is placed BEFORE `writePRMergedAudit`, which DEFERS the `pr_merged` row from merge-observation time to resolution time so a held run does not write a duplicate `pr_merged` row on every re-poll tick. A GitHub-merged run that is held by this gate therefore has NO `pr_merged` row until the re-poll clears the gate; the backstop guarantees the run always eventually resolves, so `pr_merged` is always eventually recorded. **Accepted limitations** (mirror ADR-036 / #862): the terminal count is run-wide rather than head-SHA-matched (exact for the common `agent=1` case; squash-merge SHA divergence makes per-SHA matching unreliable), and this gate observes a merge GitHub already performed — it refuses to mark the run `succeeded` but does not block the GitHub-side merge; the cosmetic dangling-open-PR residue is tracked in #877. The `merged==false` (closed-without-merge → `cancelled`) branch is **never** gated — an abandoned PR is terminal regardless of review state.

### 4.2.1 Implement-review-agent invocation (ADR-027 impl 2/2)

The same review-agent machinery extends to the **implement** stage: after the implement-stage diff lands in the trace bundle, if the implement stage's `reviewers.agent > 0`, the backend spawns that many review agents against the diff + approved plan + issue. This reuses the verdict shape, `ResolveAuthority` table, self-review WARN, and `PlanReviewer` interface verbatim — no interface change. The single new prompt variant is `implement_review` (`buildImplementReview` in `backend/internal/prompt/prompt.go`).

**Where the hook runs.** Unlike plan-review (which runs in `handleShipPlan`), implement-review runs in the **trace handler** (`backend/internal/server/trace.go::advanceStageAfterTrace`), because the diff is what's reviewed and it arrives via the trace bundle (the same `bundle.ExtractDiff` the policy re-eval uses, so it works in local `--no-pr` mode too — the diff lives in the trace regardless of whether a PR was opened). Ordering within the trace handler:

1. Manifest `agent_failed` check (fail-A short-circuit) → policy re-evaluation (`reEvaluatePolicy`).
2. `advanceStageAfterTrace`: `dispatched → running`.
3. **Implement-review hook** (guarded by `stage.Type == implement`): extract the diff via `bundle.ExtractDiff`; if extraction errors, skip review and proceed. Otherwise call `runImplementReviews`.
4. Terminal transition: `running → awaiting_approval` (or `succeeded` for gateless).

The hook is placed between step 2 and step 4 so a **gating** reject fails the stage to `failed` (category B) *before* the terminal transition — the terminal `awaiting_approval` advance is then blocked by the state machine, exactly mirroring `runPlanReviews`' contract. **Advisory** implement review is dispatched asynchronously (#584; see **Review dispatch: advisory-async vs. gating-sync** above): `runImplementReviews` returns `false` immediately and the terminal transition proceeds while the detached reviewer runs, so the upload is never held open for an advisory review. When `stage.Type != implement` or `reviewers.agent == 0`, the hook is a no-op and the trace path is byte-identical to the pre-ADR-027 flow.

**`runImplementReviews`** (`backend/internal/server/trace.go`) mirrors `runPlanReviews`: it resolves the implement stage's `ReviewersConfig` via the shared `resolveStageReviewers(runRow, spec.StageTypeImplement)`, loads the approved plan (self-review guard + prompt context), builds the `implement_review` prompt with the changed-files diff summary, invokes `PlanReviewer.Review` per configured agent, WARNs on self-review (the approved plan's `GeneratedBy.Model` is used as an approximation of the implement author's model — v0 does not record the implement agent's model separately), and appends one `implement_reviewed` audit entry per invocation. It returns `true` (gating block) only when authority is gating (`agent>0 && human==0`) and at least one verdict is `reject`.

**Decomposition fan-out review coverage (#677)**: `runImplementReviews` carries **no** `decomposed_from` guard — it fires on every implement-stage trace upload and resolves reviewers from the child run's own `WorkflowSpec` via `resolveStageReviewers(StageTypeImplement)`. So each fan-out child slice **already** receives its own per-child implement-review (with the #703 orthogonal lens) whenever the workflow's implement stage configures reviewers — no new code; the acceptance criterion "a fan-out child slice receives at least one advisory review" is satisfied by the existing machinery, not deferred (verified live: child run `1cd5ae2e`, sub-plan 1 of #688, emitted `implement_review_started → implement_reviewed(approve_with_concerns)` on 2026-06-02). What a child gets **no** per-child equivalent of is the **plan** review — by design it has no plan stage: the parent plan is reviewed pre-fan-out, and (per #677) the parent's binding approval-conditions now propagate into each child implement prompt via `resolveApprovalConditions` (see "Per-stage prompt construction"). The parent's own review stage remains the post-`children_settled` aggregate gate over the combined child branch.

**Gating consolidated review on the parent (#1060)**: per-child reviews are advisory-to-the-slice early signal — none of them sees the *consolidated* diff that actually merges, so a child-raised high never gated the parent merge and drive advanced `review:awaiting_approval → awaiting_merge` on vacuously-terminal review evidence (zero reviewers ever ran against the parent). Two coupled pieces close this. (1) **Drive safety** (slice 1): `implementReviewRound` now resolves the configured agent count from the spec even when no `implement_review_started` round was dispatched, so `ObserveParkedReviewForDrive` can distinguish a genuinely reviewer-less run (`configured==0`, vacuously terminal — still advances, never wedges) from a parent with reviewers configured but no round yet dispatched (`configured>0 && !started`, non-terminal — parks instead of stamping `checks_green_awaiting_merge`). (2) **Consolidated dispatch** (slice 2): when `orchestrator.Advance` dispatches a decomposed parent's review stage WITH the consolidated PR present, it fires `ConsolidatedReviewDispatcher` (interface field on the orchestrator, satisfied server-side by `*server.Server` to keep the dependency direction `server→orchestrator` and avoid an import cycle). `Server.DispatchConsolidatedReview` sources the consolidated diff via `githubclient.ComparePatch(base=DefaultRef, head=consolidatedBranch(parentID))` and runs `runImplementReviews` against it with the stageID of the **parent's implement stage** (the `awaiting_children→succeeded` stage). That stage id is the load-bearing seam: the `implement_review_started`/`implement_reviewed` round and any concerns attach there, so the round is non-vacuous for the drive gate AND `fishhawk_fixup_stage` on that stage resolves the consolidated concern (same `(run, stage)` concern store, no new fix-up code path). The dispatch is best-effort + idempotent (runs detached on `bgReviews`; `runImplementReviews`' started-key guard dedups the sweeper/event-driven double-advance) and no-ops for ordinary runs (a `decomposed_from==nil` run with a PR but no children). A GitHub-truncated consolidated diff (the large-diff fan-out case — the 300-file cap or an omitted oversized patch) is surfaced loudly: a WARN log plus a durable `consolidated_review_diff_truncated` audit entry (system actor, payload `{reason, changed_files}`, attached to the parent implement stage), and the review still runs on the partial diff rather than aborting. **Parent fix-up re-invokes the shared branch (#1063)**: a fix-up on a decomposed parent's implement stage re-opens it to `pending`, where `Advance` re-enters `fanoutIfDecomposed`. That helper is now guarded on existing children — when the parent already has minted children (a `ListRuns(DecomposedFrom==parent, Limit:1)` probe returns ≥1), it skips the fan-out and returns `(false, nil)` so `Advance` falls through to `dispatchStage` and re-invokes the parent's implement stage against the existing shared branch rather than re-minting a fresh fan-out. The fix-up branch derivation matches: `server.fixupBranchForRun` resolves a decomposed PARENT (`DecomposedFrom==nil` WITH minted children) to the consolidated/shared branch `fishhawk/run-<shortID(runID)>` (byte-matching `orchestrator.consolidatedBranch`) instead of the per-stage branch, so the fix-up commit lands on the consolidated PR head. Only the first fan-out (zero children) mints; the concern-attach + resolve seam is unchanged.

**Review lens is re-aimed at the orthogonal gaps** (#703): the `implement_review` prompt no longer re-verifies plan adherence or generic-bug-hunts — mechanical correctness is already gated by the policy gate, the implement agent's test run, build/lint, and CI, so re-stating it adds no signal. Instead it applies the three lenses the deterministic gates are blind to: security / authz (anchored to Fishhawk's code-execution threat model — lethal trifecta / uncontrolled egress, ADR-029 / #650), test vacuity (a test that asserts nothing load-bearing still passes CI), and untested error / edge / concurrency paths. The security lens is **self-gating**: on a low-risk diff that touches no sensitive surface it states so briefly and stops rather than manufacturing a concern. The reviewer stays advisory (no authority change); the grounded-citations and style-is-lint criteria (#595) are retained.

**scope.files drift is flag-only** (ADR-027 Decision Q6): the prompt instructs the review agent to emit a `{category: "scope"}` concern when the diff touches files outside the plan's `scope.files`, but drift alone never forces a `reject`. Only an overall verdict of `reject` blocks under gating authority. A reviewer returning `approve_with_concerns` with only a scope concern advances the stage.

**Operator-stageable scope_drift is surfaced to the reviewer** (#695, #581): the review's `git_diff` is **scope-bounded** — it excludes paths the implement stage dirtied but did not declare, which the operator may `git add` into the final commit. The runner already emits those paths as a `scope_drift` `policy_event` in the trace bundle (from `computeAndEmitDiff`); the trace handler now reads them via `bundle.ExtractScopeDrift` and threads them onto `prompt.Trigger.ScopeDrift`, so `buildImplementReview` renders a "Scope drift (excluded from the diff above — operator may stage)" section naming each path. This stops the advisory reviewer false-rejecting a required test/doc as "absent from the committed artifact" when it actually landed via a drifted path. A **standing anti-false-reject rule** backs this up even when the event is absent or stripped (best-effort extraction degrades to `nil`): the reviewer must treat an absence it cannot positively confirm as unverifiable (→ `approve_with_concerns`), never confirmed-missing. The reviewer sees only the drifted path **names**, not their content (the bundle's scoped diff excludes drift content), so it can flag-vs-false-reject but cannot test-vacuity-check a drifted test file — an inherent limit of the backend-only approach.

**Machine-verified gate evidence is surfaced to the reviewer** (#963): the runner appends a single synthesized `gate_evidence` trace event (kind `gate_evidence`, composed by `composeGateEvidence` immediately before `bundle.PackBytes` so BOTH variants carry it) digesting the stage's deterministic gate results: per-`verify_run` command/exit/outcome with a bounded (last 30 lines, ~4KB) output tail, the `verify_summary` outcome/iterations/detail, `verify_infra_flake_retry` count, scope-enforcement facts (declared `scope.files` count, staged-file count, the `scope_drift` undeclared list), and constraint-violation `policy_events`. Every free-text field is pre-redacted runner-side via `redaction.RedactDefault` (the shared `redaction/` module, #1106) — required by ordering (the implement review dispatches on the RAW variant, before the redacted variant exists); the raw `verify_run` events keep their full unredacted output for compliance, only the derived digest is prompt-bound.
